categories.name: Shapes & Geometry
categories.description: Foundational geometric forms used in logos, icons, layouts, and decorative design.

prompts.textual: Create a perfect circle element with a vibrant purple-to-pink gradient fill. Use it as a decorative accent or avatar placeholder. Ensure smooth anti-aliased edges.
Create a square shape with slightly rounded corners (4px radius) and a teal-to-blue gradient. Use as a card thumbnail or icon container.
Create a rounded rectangle (pill-adjacent shape) with generous corner radius (12px+). Fill with a warm orange-to-pink gradient. Great for buttons, tags, and cards.

patterns.name: Triangle

prompts.textual: Create an equilateral triangle pointing upward with a solid purple fill. Use for directional indicators, play buttons, or geometric pattern elements.

patterns.name: Inverted Triangle

prompts.textual: Create a downward-pointing triangle in pink. Useful for dropdown indicators, funnel graphics, or decorative geometric elements.
Create a diamond/rhombus shape (a square rotated 45°) with an amber/gold fill. Use for decorative bullet points, status indicators, or geometric patterns.
Create a regular pentagon shape with a mint/teal fill. Use in geometric pattern designs, infographic elements, or as a unique container shape.
prompts.html:
<!-- HTML/CSS implementation for Pentagon -->
<svg width="64" height="64" viewBox="0 0 64 64"><polygon points="32,4 60,24 50,56 14,56 4,24" fill="#5cfcb6"/></svg>

Create a semicircle (half-circle) with a teal fill, flat edge at the bottom. Use for tab indicators, decorative dividers, or gauge charts.
Create a quarter circle (90° arc) shape. Use for pie chart segments, corner decorations, or radial menu elements.
Create a parallelogram by skewing a rectangle. Use for dynamic, motion-implied shapes in banners, tech graphics, or racing-themed designs.
Create a trapezoid shape (wider at top or bottom). Use for perspective effects, platform/stage graphics, or funnel diagram steps.
Create a plus/cross shape with rounded ends in mint green. Use for add buttons, medical/health icons, or decorative geometric patterns.

categories.name: Lines & Strokes
categories.description: Different line styles and stroke treatments used in illustration, UI, and decorative design.

prompts.textual: Draw a solid, continuous line with a 2-4px weight in purple. Use for separators, underlines, or structural borders.
Create a dashed line (alternating dash and gap) in pink. Use for cut lines, secondary separators, placeholder borders, or movement paths.
Create a dotted line (series of small round dots) in teal. Use for subtle separators, fold indicators, or decorative dividers.
Create a double line (two parallel strokes with a gap between). Use for formal dividers, certificate borders, or elegant section separators.
Create a line with a multi-color gradient flowing from purple through pink to amber. Use for progress bars, decorative accents, or branded dividers.

categories.name: Gradients & Color Fills
categories.description: Color transition techniques that add depth, dimension, and visual interest.

prompts.textual: Create a linear gradient transitioning from purple (top-left) to pink (bottom-right) at a 135° angle. Use for backgrounds, buttons, or hero sections.
Create a radial gradient emanating from center: amber core fading through pink to purple at edges. Use for spotlight effects, orbs, or glowing elements.
Create a conic (angular/sweep) gradient cycling through purple, teal, amber, pink, and back to purple around 360°. Use for color wheels, loading spinners, or vibrant decorative elements.
Apply a duotone effect using purple highlights and pink shadows. Map the image's light values to one color and dark values to another. Popular in Spotify-style branding and editorial design.

Create a mesh gradient with multiple color points: purple, teal, pink, and amber blending organically across the surface. Use for modern hero backgrounds and creative branding. Tools like Figma and CSS Houdini support true mesh gradients.

patterns.name: Noise Gradient

prompts.textual: Create a gradient from purple to pink with a subtle film-grain/noise texture overlay. The grain adds tactile warmth and reduces banding. Use for editorial backgrounds, album art, or vintage-modern aesthetics.
Use a solid, flat color fill (#7C5CFC purple). Clean and decisive. Use for buttons, icons, or minimalist design elements where simplicity is key.

Create a glassmorphism fill: semi-transparent background (white or colored at 10-20% opacity) with backdrop-filter blur (8-16px), a subtle white border (15% opacity), and a soft drop shadow. Creates a frosted-glass effect.
Apply a semi-transparent color overlay (purple at 50% opacity) on top of an image or pattern. Use for darkening photos for text readability, brand-tinting images, or creating mood effects.
Create a hard-edge split between two colors at the midpoint — purple on the left, amber on the right. Use for bold, graphic compositions, before/after comparisons, or striking branding.

Apply a gradient map — remap the tonal range of an image to a custom gradient. Dark pixels map to the left color, light pixels to the right. Creates stylized, branded imagery. Use in Photoshop (Gradient Map adjustment) or CSS with mix-blend-mode tricks.

Create an easing gradient — instead of linear interpolation between two colors, use multiple stops to create an ease-in-out curve. Prevents the muddy gray band that appears in linear gradients between certain color pairs. More natural and pleasing transitions.

Create a metallic gradient — multiple alternating light and dark stops simulating polished metal reflections. Use silver (grays), gold (ambers), copper (warm browns), or rose gold tones. Apply to text, icons, or shapes for a premium, luxurious appearance.

Create a striped/hard-stop gradient — use identical percentage values at color transitions to create sharp bands instead of smooth blending. Creates bold, graphic stripes. Use for progress bars, decorative backgrounds, or retro-themed designs.

Create an animated gradient — a gradient with background-size larger than the element (200-400%) that animates background-position to create a flowing, shifting color effect. Use for premium CTAs, loading states, or hero backgrounds. Subtle and mesmerizing.

categories.name: Shadows & Depth
categories.description: Techniques to create the illusion of depth, elevation, and light interaction.

prompts.textual: Add a drop shadow (0 4px 12px rgba(0,0,0,0.3)) to create a floating card effect. This is the most common depth technique in UI design — simulates a light source above.
Add a large, soft, diffused shadow with high blur radius (32px+) and low opacity. Creates a gentle, ambient floating effect. Popular in modern UI design for cards and modals.
Add a hard shadow with zero blur — a solid offset block. Use a darker shade of the element's color. Creates a bold, graphic, retro-poster aesthetic. Popular in brutalist and neo-retro design.
Apply an inset/inner shadow to make the element appear recessed or carved into the surface. Use for input fields, wells, or pressed-button states.

Create layered shadows — stack 3+ box-shadows at increasing offsets and blur radii with decreasing opacity. This produces the most realistic, natural-looking elevation. Used in Material Design and modern UI systems.
Create a long shadow extending at 45° from the element, fading out over a long distance. Classic flat design technique from the 2013 era. Use for icon design and retro-flat aesthetics.
Add a colored outer glow using box-shadow with 0 offset and large spread + blur in the element's color. Creates a luminous, neon-like aura. Use for highlights, active states, or sci-fi aesthetics.

Create a neon glow effect: element in a bright color (#5CFCB6 teal) with multiple layered box-shadows at increasing blur radii in the same color. Creates the look of a neon tube sign. Great for dark-theme designs, cyberpunk aesthetics, or nightlife branding.
Apply a colored text shadow to typography — use a glow-like shadow (0 2px 8px) in a brand color behind white text. Adds depth and emphasis to headlines on dark backgrounds.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Create a neumorphic (soft UI) shadow: pair a dark shadow (bottom-right) with a lighter highlight shadow (top-left) on a similar-colored background. The element appears softly extruded from the surface. Use for toggles, buttons, or dashboard widgets.

Create a reflection effect below an element — a vertically flipped, faded copy with a gradient mask from partial opacity to transparent. Use for product showcases, hero elements, or glossy surface simulations.

Add a colored shadow matching the element's fill color — instead of black/gray, use the element's dominant color at 30-40% opacity. Creates a more cohesive, modern look. The element appears to emit light onto the surface below. Standard in modern UI design.

Add an ambient/contact shadow — an elliptical shadow directly beneath an element, simulating its contact with the surface. Smaller and more focused than drop shadows. Use for floating elements, product photography, or elements that appear lifted off the page.

Add a perspective/cast shadow — a shadow that extends at an angle, simulating a light source from one side. Use transform: skewX() on a pseudo-element. More dramatic and realistic than standard drop shadows. Great for hero elements or featured cards.

Create a cutout/letterpress effect — text or shapes appear pressed into the surface. Use a color matching the background with a subtle light text-shadow from below. Creates a debossed, stamped-in look. Use for subtle watermarks, embossed cards, or premium textures.
prompts.html: Deboss

categories.name: Borders & Outlines
categories.description: Edge treatments that define, contain, and decorate elements.

prompts.textual: Add a solid 2px border in purple around the element. The most fundamental edge treatment — provides clean containment.
Create a gradient border using the background-clip trick: set background to the fill on padding-box and the gradient on border-box with a transparent border. Creates a multicolor border effect.
Add a double border — two parallel lines with a gap. Use border-style: double. Adds formality and elegance. Great for certificate borders or premium UI elements.
Add a dashed border in amber. Use for drop zones, upload areas, placeholder containers, or indicating an element can be interacted with.
Add a dotted border in pink. More decorative than dashed — use for coupon cut-lines, soft containers, or whimsical card frames.
Add an outline with offset — a border floating 4px outside the element. Critical for accessible focus indicators. Use contrasting colors for visibility.
Apply a border-image with a repeating gradient creating a candy-stripe pattern. Use for festive, playful borders or to add visual interest to otherwise plain containers.
Create a beveled/3D border by using different colors for each edge — lighter on top/left, darker on bottom/right. Creates an old-school raised or sunken look. Classic in retro UI and game interfaces.

Create an animated border — a conic gradient rotating around the element's edge using @keyframes to rotate the angle. Creates a spinning, glowing border effect. Use for featured cards, active elements, or premium UI components. Apply with background conic-gradient on a wrapper with inner padding.

Create a notched/perforated border — circular cutouts along the edge of a card, like a ticket or coupon. Use pseudo-elements or overlapping circles matching the background. Classic for event tickets, coupons, boarding passes, or receipt-style designs.

Create an inset border using box-shadow: inset 0 0 0 2px — the border sits inside the element rather than adding to its dimensions. Avoids layout shifts that regular borders cause. Use for hover states, selected items, or anywhere borders shouldn't affect sizing.

Create a scalloped/wavy edge — a repeating semicircle pattern along one or more edges of an element. Use SVG paths or radial-gradient masking. Creates a decorative, craft-like border. Use for greeting cards, invitations, doily patterns, or playful UI containers.
prompts.html:
<!-- HTML/CSS implementation for Scalloped Edge -->

patterns.name: Corner Accent

prompts.textual: Create corner accent brackets — small L-shaped lines at each corner instead of a full border. Elegant and minimal. Use for image frames, feature sections, quotes, or viewfinder-style focus indicators. Suggests containment without fully enclosing.

categories.name: Typography Styles
categories.description: Font classifications and typographic treatments that define the voice of design.

prompts.textual: Use a serif typeface (e.g., Georgia, Times New Roman, Playfair Display). Serifs have small decorative strokes at letter terminals. Convey tradition, authority, elegance. Use for editorial headlines, luxury brands, or formal documents.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a sans-serif typeface (e.g., Helvetica, Inter, SF Pro). No decorative strokes — clean and modern. Convey clarity, simplicity, tech-forward. Default for digital UI, apps, and modern branding.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a monospace/fixed-width typeface (e.g., Fira Code, JetBrains Mono, Courier). Every character occupies the same width. Use for code blocks, technical data, terminal aesthetics, or retro-tech branding.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a script/cursive typeface with flowing, connected letterforms that mimic calligraphy or handwriting. Convey elegance, creativity, personal touch. Use for wedding invitations, beauty brands, or signature elements.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a display/decorative typeface designed for large sizes and headlines (e.g., Impact, Abril Fatface). High visual impact with unique character shapes. Use for posters, headlines, logos — NOT body text.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a slab serif typeface with thick, block-like serifs (e.g., Roboto Slab, Rockwell, Zilla Slab). Combine serif authority with modern boldness. Use for strong headlines, tech brands, or editorial pull quotes.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a condensed/narrow typeface with compressed letter widths (e.g., Barlow Condensed, Oswald). Fit more text in less space while maintaining impact. Use for headlines in tight layouts, sports graphics, or news design.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use an extended/wide typeface or increase letter-spacing significantly. Creates spacious, luxurious feel. Use for fashion branding, architectural signage, or premium product labels.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Create outlined/hollow typography — text with only a stroke/border and no fill. Use -webkit-text-stroke or SVG. Modern and airy. Use for large display text, layered headlines, or secondary emphasis.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Apply a gradient fill to text — use background-clip: text with a linear gradient. Creates vibrant, eye-catching headlines. Popular in tech branding (Instagram, Apple). Use for hero text or key CTAs.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Set text in ALL CAPS with increased letter-spacing (tracking). Conveys authority and structure. Use for labels, navigation, section headings, or brand names. Pair with generous tracking for readability.
prompts.html: Label

prompts.textual: Use small caps — uppercase letterforms at lowercase height. More refined than all-caps. Use for bylines, subheadings, legal text, or elegant UI labels. Apply with font-variant: small-caps.
prompts.html: Small Caps

prompts.textual: Create a drop cap — an oversized first letter of a paragraph that spans 2-3 lines of body text. Use for editorial layouts, articles, or storybook aesthetics. Float the letter left and size it 3-4x the body text.
prompts.html: Once upon a design...

prompts.textual: Create knockout/mask text where the letterforms cut through a foreground layer to reveal an image or gradient behind. The text acts as a window. Use for dramatic hero sections or creative headers using mix-blend-mode or background-clip.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a Blackletter/Old English typeface with ornate, angular letterforms derived from medieval calligraphy. Conveys tradition, authority, and formality (or rebellion in modern contexts). Use for beer/brewery branding, heavy metal, luxury fashion, newspaper mastheads, or certificate titles.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use a stencil typeface — letterforms with breaks/gaps in the strokes as if cut from a physical stencil template. Conveys military, industrial, or utilitarian aesthetics. Use for crate markings, army-themed design, construction branding, or street-art inspired layouts.
prompts.html: ABC

prompts.textual: Create inline/layered type — text with multiple offset shadows or duplicated layers creating a dimensional, vintage poster effect. Layer a highlight offset and a shadow offset in contrasting tones. Use for retro headlines, circus/carnival themes, or decorative display text.
prompts.html: Aa

prompts.textual: Use superscript (raised) and subscript (lowered) text for mathematical formulas, chemical notation, footnote markers, ordinal indicators (1st, 2nd), or trademark symbols (™). Use HTML <sup>/<sub> tags or vertical-align with reduced font size. Maintain readable size.
prompts.html: H2O & E=mc2

prompts.textual: Apply strikethrough (text-decoration: line-through) to indicate deleted, outdated, or corrected content. Use a contrasting color for the line. Common for showing original vs. sale prices, completed tasks, or edits in collaborative documents.
prompts.html: $99 $49

categories.description: Repeating motifs and surface treatments that add richness and tactility.

prompts.textual: Create a polka dot pattern — evenly spaced circles on a contrasting background. Use radial-gradient for CSS. Conveys playfulness and retro charm. Use for backgrounds, textiles, or whimsical branding.
Create a diagonal stripe pattern with repeating-linear-gradient at 45°. Alternating colored and transparent bands. Use for hazard indicators, decorative backgrounds, or barbershop-style branding.
Create a checkerboard pattern — alternating colored squares in a grid. Classic transparency indicator in design tools. Use for racing themes, retro aesthetics, or as a subtle background texture.
Create a grid/graph-paper pattern with thin lines forming squares. Use for technical backgrounds, wireframe aesthetics, data visualization canvases, or engineering-themed designs.

categories.name: Visual Effects & Filters
categories.description: Post-processing effects, blurs, and visual treatments that transform elements.

prompts.textual: Apply a Gaussian blur (filter: blur(6px)) to soften an element. Creates dreamy, out-of-focus effect. Use for background layers, depth-of-field simulation, or obscuring content behind overlays.
Simulate motion blur — directional smearing that implies speed. Use a combination of directional shadow and slight blur. Apply to elements in transition, sports graphics, or action-themed designs.

Apply backdrop-filter: blur() to create a frosted glass effect — the content behind the element becomes blurred while the element itself stays sharp. Foundation of glassmorphism. Use for overlays, navigation bars, and modals.

Apply grayscale filter (filter: grayscale(1)) to remove all color saturation. Creates monochrome elegance. Use for disabled states, background images, hover-off states (color on hover), or dramatic editorial photos.
Apply sepia filter for a warm, vintage photograph look — tinted brown/amber. Use for nostalgic themes, historical content, or aged-paper aesthetics.

Increase color saturation (filter: saturate(2)) to make colors more vivid and intense. Use for eye-catching hero images, psychedelic effects, or emphasizing vibrancy. Use sparingly — oversaturation looks unnatural.

Apply hue-rotate (filter: hue-rotate(90deg)) to shift all colors around the color wheel. Use for creating color variations from a single source, psychedelic effects, or animated color-cycling transitions.
Apply color inversion (filter: invert(1)) to reverse all colors — light becomes dark, blue becomes orange, etc. Use for dark mode toggles, high-contrast accessibility modes, or glitch art effects.
prompts.html: →

prompts.textual: Use mix-blend-mode (screen, multiply, overlay, etc.) to blend overlapping elements. Screen lightens, multiply darkens, overlay combines both. Use for creative image overlays, duotone effects, and artistic compositions.

Add a vignette — darkened edges fading to transparent at center using radial-gradient. Draws the eye to the center of the composition. Classic photographic technique. Use for hero images, cards, or cinematic mood.

Create a glitch effect — offset duplicate layers of text/image with color channel separation (red/cyan or pink/teal). Use clip-path to slice horizontal bands. Conveys digital disruption, cyberpunk aesthetic, or tech-edge branding.
prompts.html: GlitchGlitchGlitch

patterns.name: Noise / Static

prompts.textual: Create a TV static/digital noise effect — high-frequency random pixel noise covering the element. Use SVG feTurbulence with high baseFrequency. Animate by changing the seed value each frame. Use for retro-tech, VHS aesthetics, horror themes, or signal-loss transitions.

Add CRT scanlines — thin horizontal lines overlaying content, simulating an old CRT monitor or TV screen. Use repeating-linear-gradient with alternating transparent and semi-black bands. Use for retro-gaming, cyberpunk, VHS/synthwave, or vintage-tech aesthetics.

Create a chromatic aberration effect — separate the red, green, and blue color channels with slight position offsets. Creates a lens distortion look. Use layered elements with color mix-blend-mode or offset colored shadows. Use for photography, glitch art, or cinematic UI.
prompts.html: TextTextText

prompts.textual: Apply pixelation/mosaic effect — reduce the image resolution to create visible blocky pixels. Use CSS image-rendering: pixelated on scaled-down images, or canvas-based pixel manipulation. Use for retro-game aesthetics, privacy blurring (faces), or transitional loading effects.

Create an emboss/deboss effect — element appears raised from or pressed into the surface. Use dual opposing shadows — light from top-left, dark from bottom-right (emboss) or reversed (deboss). The element color matches the background. Classic for premium print simulation.
prompts.html: Emboss

prompts.textual: Create a frosted/feathered edge — content fades to transparent at the edges using CSS mask-image with a gradient mask. Use for horizontal scrolling containers (fade indicators), image edges, or blending elements into backgrounds. Smoother than hard clip-path edges.

Create a bloom/glow effect — bright areas of an element bleed softly into surrounding pixels, as if captured by a camera sensor. Simulate by layering a blurred, enlarged copy of the bright color behind the sharp element. Use for neon UI, sci‑fi HUDs, or emphasizing key lights in dark interfaces.

Create a tilt‑shift depth effect — sharp focus band across the center with heavy blur above and below, making scenes look like miniatures. Use layered blurred copies plus mask-image to control which strip stays in focus. Great for storytelling hero imagery and map/product shots with a focal strip.

Create a long-shadow effect — a flat icon or shape with an exaggerated diagonal shadow extending away from a consistent light source. Use a gradient or multiple box-shadows at 45°. Popular in flat design, retro posters, and dashboard icons where you want depth without full realism.

categories.name: UI Design Styles
categories.description: Distinct visual paradigms and aesthetic movements in interface design.

prompts.textual: Design a glassmorphism-style card: semi-transparent background (rgba white 8-12%), backdrop-filter: blur(12px), thin white border at 12% opacity, soft shadow. The content behind shows through with a frosted effect. Popular in macOS Big Sur, Windows 11.

Design a neumorphic element: match the background color of the surface, then apply dual shadows — dark (bottom-right) and light (top-left). The element appears to extrude from the surface. Use for buttons, toggles, input fields. Best on simple, single-color backgrounds.

Design a claymorphism element: rounded shape with inner shadows that create a soft, inflated 3D appearance — like a clay model. Use light inner shadow on top, dark inner shadow on bottom, plus an external shadow. Playful and tactile.

Design in brutalist style: raw, bold, unpolished. Use harsh shadows, thick black borders, system fonts, bright clashing colors, and deliberate "ugliness." Reject smooth gradients and subtle effects. Inspired by Craigslist, Bloomberg.com aesthetics.
prompts.html: RAW

prompts.textual: Design in flat style: solid colors, no gradients, no shadows, no textures. Clean geometric shapes, bold typography, bright color palette. Popularized by Windows Metro and iOS 7+. Focus on content and typography hierarchy.

Design following Material Design principles: paper-like surfaces with realistic shadows indicating elevation (dp), smooth motion, bold color accents, 8dp grid system. Elements cast shadows based on their z-axis position. Use Google's elevation system.

Create an aurora/northern-lights UI effect: soft, overlapping gradient blobs with large blur radii that create an ambient, ethereal glow. Use multiple semi-transparent colored circles with heavy blur. Dreamy, cosmic aesthetic.

patterns.name: Retro / Pixel Art

prompts.textual: Create a pixel art / retro 8-bit style design with visible pixel grid, limited color palette (4-8 colors), no anti-aliasing, and blocky shapes. Use for game-themed designs, nostalgic branding, or retro UI elements. Each "pixel" should be a distinct square.

Design in skeuomorphic style — mimic real-world materials and physics. Use realistic textures (leather, metal, wood), reflections, glossy highlights, and physical depth. Elements should look like their real-world counterparts. Think early iOS (pre-iOS 7), with stitching, page curls, and realistic buttons.

Design in Frutiger Aero style (mid-2000s to early 2010s tech aesthetic). Use glossy textures, heavy glass reflections, bright sky blues and grass greens, aurora borealis effects, water droplets, and lens flares. Highly optimistic, shiny, and hyper-polished.

Design a Bento Box UI — a strict, modular grid layout where content is divided into distinct, rounded-corner rectangles (cards) of varying sizes, fitting perfectly together like a Japanese bento box. Popularized by Apple. Great for dashboards, portfolios, and feature highlight sections.

Design in Cyberpunk style — dark backgrounds, glowing neon accents (cyan, magenta, yellow), monospaced typography, grid lines, crosshairs, glitch effects, and complex HUD-like data visualizations. Evokes a high-tech, dystopian future.

prompts.textual: Create a toggle switch — a pill-shaped track with a circular thumb that slides between on/off states. Active state in brand color, inactive in gray. Use for boolean settings like dark mode, notifications, or feature toggles.

Create a progress bar — a horizontal track with a colored fill indicating completion percentage. Round both track and fill. Use gradient fill for visual interest. Include for file uploads, onboarding steps, or loading states.

Create a skeleton loader — a content placeholder that mimics the layout of real content using animated gray blocks. Apply a shimmer animation (moving highlight gradient) across the placeholders. Use while data is loading to reduce perceived wait time.

Create breadcrumb navigation showing the page hierarchy path. Each level is a clickable link (except current page) separated by "/" or ">" chevrons. Use for deep page structures. Helps users orient themselves and navigate back up.

categories.name: Color Theory & Harmony
categories.description: Color relationships and schemes that create visual cohesion and emotion.

prompts.textual: Create a monochromatic color palette — variations of a single hue at different lightness and saturation levels. Produces a cohesive, sophisticated look. Use for minimalist designs, corporate branding, or elegant presentations.

Use complementary colors — two hues directly opposite on the color wheel (e.g., purple and yellow-green). Creates maximum contrast and visual vibration. Use for CTAs that need to pop, accent colors, or energetic designs. Handle with care — can be overwhelming.

Use analogous colors — 3-4 hues adjacent on the color wheel (e.g., blue → purple → pink). Creates harmonious, serene palettes with natural flow. Use for gradient backgrounds, nature themes, or cohesive brand systems.

Use a triadic color scheme — three colors equally spaced (120° apart) on the color wheel (e.g., purple, orange, green). Vibrant and balanced. Use for playful brands, children's products, or dynamic infographics. Let one color dominate, use others as accents.

Use a split-complementary scheme — a base color plus the two colors adjacent to its complement. Less tension than pure complementary, more nuance. Easier to work with while still providing strong contrast.

Use a warm color palette — reds, oranges, yellows, and warm neutrals. Evokes energy, passion, warmth, and urgency. Use for food brands, sale CTAs, or cozy/inviting interfaces. Warm colors advance visually — they feel closer.

Use a cool color palette — blues, teals, cyans, and cool greens. Evokes calm, trust, professionalism, and spaciousness. Use for tech brands, healthcare, finance, or meditation apps. Cool colors recede — they create depth.

Use a neutral color palette — grays, beiges, taupes, off-whites, and muted earth tones. Sophisticated, timeless, and versatile. Use for luxury brands, minimalist design, or as a base palette that lets accent colors shine.

Use a pastel palette — soft, desaturated tints with high lightness. Gentle, approachable, and calming. Use for baby products, wellness brands, spring themes, or friendly SaaS interfaces. Pair with one bold accent for hierarchy.

Use a tetradic (rectangle/square) color scheme — four colors forming a rectangle on the color wheel (two complementary pairs). Rich and complex. Use one dominant color, two supporting, and one accent. Requires careful balance to avoid being chaotic. Best for complex, feature-rich interfaces.

Use an achromatic (colorless) palette — exclusively grays, blacks, and whites with no hue. Creates sophisticated, elegant, and timeless designs. Forces reliance on typography, spacing, and imagery for visual interest. Use for luxury brands, portfolios, or editorial design. Add one accent color for CTAs.

Use a neon/fluorescent palette — extremely saturated, high-energy colors that appear to glow. Best on dark backgrounds. Add glow effects via box-shadow. Use for nightclub themes, gaming, cyberpunk, Gen-Z targeting, or event promotions. Use sparingly — high visual intensity.

Use earth tone colors — browns, olives, terra cottas, sage greens, and warm tans drawn from natural landscapes. Grounded, organic, and calming. Use for outdoor brands, organic food, sustainable products, wellness, or farmhouse-style design.

Use jewel tone colors — deep, saturated hues inspired by gemstones: amethyst purple, emerald green, ruby red, sapphire blue, topaz gold. Rich and opulent. Use for luxury brands, premium products, evening event invitations, or high-end editorial design.

categories.name: Composition & Layout
categories.description: Spatial arrangement principles that guide the viewer's eye and create visual hierarchy.

prompts.textual: Apply the rule of thirds — divide the canvas into a 3×3 grid and place key elements at the intersection points or along the grid lines. Creates naturally balanced, dynamic compositions. Place the focal point at one of the four intersection points rather than dead center.

prompts.textual: Create a symmetrical layout — mirror elements evenly across a central axis. Conveys stability, formality, and order. Use for landing page heroes, logos, or any design requiring classical balance and authority.

Create an asymmetrical layout — deliberate imbalance where visual weight is distributed unevenly but still feels balanced. More dynamic and modern than symmetry. Use for editorial layouts, portfolios, or creative sites. Balance through contrast in size, color, or position.

prompts.textual: Establish clear visual hierarchy — use size, weight, color, and spacing to signal the importance of each element. The most important element should be the largest/boldest. Progress from primary (large, bold, high contrast) through secondary to tertiary (smaller, lighter, less contrast).

Subtitle
Body text goes here...

prompts.textual: Use generous white space (negative space) — empty areas around and between elements. Don't fill every pixel. White space improves readability, directs focus, creates luxury feel, and reduces cognitive load. More white space = more premium perception.
prompts.html: Less is more

prompts.textual: Design on a consistent grid system — establish column count (4, 8, or 12 columns), gutter width, and margins. All elements align to the grid. Creates order, consistency, and scalability. Use CSS Grid or Flexbox. Modular grids add row structure for magazine-style layouts.

Establish a clear focal point — the single most important element that the eye is drawn to first. Use size, contrast, color, isolation, or convergent lines to direct attention. Every composition needs a clear entry point. Secondary elements should support, not compete with, the focal point.

Create a radial composition — elements arranged in a circular pattern radiating from a central focal point. The center is the hero/key element, with supporting items orbiting around it. Use for feature showcases, team pages, menu wheels, or dashboard widgets.

Use overlapping/layered elements — intentionally stack elements so they partially cover each other. Creates depth, visual interest, and breaks rigid grid layouts. Use for hero sections (text over image), card stacks, or editorial layouts. Control z-index carefully.

Use proximity to group related content — items near each other are perceived as belonging together. Use tighter spacing (8-12px) between related items and larger spacing (24-48px) between groups. No borders or backgrounds needed — space alone creates grouping.

Create emphasis through contrast — make the important element different from its neighbors in size, color, weight, or style. The greater the contrast, the stronger the emphasis. Use for pricing table highlights, primary CTAs among secondary ones, or featured content cards.

categories.name: Website Architecture & Page Patterns
categories.description: High-level structures for how pages, navigation, and content are organized.

prompts.textual: Design a single-page narrative layout — one long page where sections (hero, problem, solution, features, proof, CTA) stack vertically. Navigation scrolls to anchors instead of loading new pages. Great for focused products, landing pages, and campaigns where you control the story arc.

Plan a multi-level sitemap — a tree of pages grouped into clear sections (e.g., Products, Solutions, Resources, Company). Each branch stays shallow (ideally 2–3 levels deep) to avoid buried content. Use this to decide global nav labels and which content deserves its own page vs a section.

patterns.name: Card-Based Grid

prompts.textual: Use card-based grids as an architecture pattern — each card is a self-contained gateway to deeper content (product, article, case study). The grid becomes the “index” page for a content type. Design consistent card anatomy: image, title, meta, CTA. Support filtering, sorting, and responsive reflow.

Use a master–detail layout — a list on the left (master) and the selected item's details on the right (detail). Avoid page reloads; selection swaps detail content. Ideal for email, file browsers, settings, and any workflow where users need to scan and inspect many items quickly.

patterns.name: Scrolling Strategies
aliases: Pagination vs infinite scroll vs load more

prompts.textual: Choose a scrolling pattern intentionally: pagination (clear sense of total, good for search/results), “Load more” button (progressive reveal with explicit control), or infinite scroll (immersive browsing but poor for tasks and footers). Match the pattern to user goals and content volume.

Create a dot grid background — evenly spaced small dots forming a subtle grid pattern. More refined than line grids. Use as a canvas/workspace background, for note-taking UI, or as a subtle texture behind content.

Create a confetti effect — scattered small rectangles, circles, and streamers in various bright colors, sizes, and rotation angles. Use for celebration screens (successful purchase, milestone), party themes, or festive landing pages. Animate with CSS or JS for falling motion.

Create a starburst/sunburst shape — alternating triangular rays emanating from a center point. Use for "Sale!", "New!", or "Special" badges, retro promotions, or eye-catching callouts. Rotate slowly for animated attention-grabbing.

prompts.textual: Create a washi tape effect — a semi-transparent, slightly rotated rectangle with jagged/torn edges overlapping a photo or card. Gives a scrapbook, handmade, or moodboard aesthetic. Great for portfolios, craft sites, or casual blog layouts.

Add digital stickers — graphics with a thick white border and drop shadow, slightly rotated, placed over other elements as if physically stuck on. Adds a playful, Gen-Z, or physical product vibe. Can be animated to "peel" on hover.
prompts.html: NEW

prompts.textual: Use grid crosshairs (small '+' marks) at the corners of containers or intersections of an invisible grid. Adds a technical, architectural, or cyberpunk aesthetic. Very popular in modern brutalist and developer-focused tool websites.

Use abstract blobs — smooth, organic, asymmetrical shapes used as background elements to break up rigid grids. Often filled with soft colors or gradients. Gives a friendly, human, and modern feel to tech products and illustrations.

Create a torn paper edge effect — a jagged, irregular border separating two sections, simulating ripped paper. Use SVG masks or clip-path. Adds texture, grunge, or a handmade feel to otherwise clean digital layouts.

Use geometric primitives — scattered circles, squares, and triangles in solid colors. Often overlapping or floating in the background. A staple of modern, clean tech illustration and abstract brand patterns.

categories.name: Branding Elements
categories.description: Core visual identity components that define a brand's look and feel.

prompts.textual: Design a wordmark/logotype — the brand name rendered in a distinctive typeface. May include custom letterforms, ligatures, or a colored accent character. Examples: Google, Coca-Cola, FedEx. The typography IS the logo. Focus on kerning, weight, and uniqueness.

prompts.textual: Design a lettermark/monogram — one or two letters (usually initials) in a distinctive container. Examples: IBM, HBO, NASA. The letter becomes an icon. Focus on the shape's relationship with its container and ensuring readability at all sizes.

patterns.name: Brand Illustration System

prompts.textual: Define a brand illustration system — a consistent visual language for all custom illustrations. Specify: color palette (subset of brand colors), stroke weight (if outlined), corner radius, level of detail, character proportions, and perspective type. All illustrations should feel like they belong to the same family.

Design a social media avatar — a circular profile image optimized for small sizes (48-128px). Often the brand's symbol on a gradient ring/border. Must be instantly recognizable at 32px. Consider the circular crop — centered, padded, high contrast. Test across platforms (different sizes, dark/light backgrounds).
prompts.html: B

prompts.textual: Create a watermark — a semi-transparent brand mark overlaid on content to indicate ownership or prevent unauthorized use. Use 3-8% opacity, rotated 15-30°, and repeated diagonally across the surface. Must be visible on close inspection but not obstruct content. Use for draft documents, stock photos, or premium content previews.
prompts.html: BRAND

categories.name: Motion & Animation
categories.description: Movement principles and timing functions that bring interfaces to life.

prompts.textual: Apply ease-in timing — animation starts slow and accelerates. Use for elements leaving the screen (exits). Cubic-bezier(0.42, 0, 1, 1). Creates the feeling of building momentum, like a ball rolling downhill.

Apply ease-out timing — animation starts fast and decelerates. Use for elements entering the screen (entrances). Cubic-bezier(0, 0, 0.58, 1). Creates the feeling of arriving and settling, like a ball rolling to a stop. Most natural for UI transitions.

Apply ease-in-out timing — slow start, fast middle, slow end. The most natural-feeling motion. Cubic-bezier(0.42, 0, 0.58, 1). Use for elements transitioning between states (open/close, expand/collapse). Smooth and polished.

Apply a bounce easing — the element overshoots its target and bounces back before settling. Playful and energetic. Use for notifications dropping in, items being added to a list, or fun micro-interactions. Not suitable for serious/professional UIs.

Create a fade transition — animate opacity from 0 to 1 (fade in) or 1 to 0 (fade out). The most subtle and versatile animation. Use for page transitions, modal appearances, tooltip reveals, or skeleton → content transitions. Duration: 150-300ms for UI elements.

Create a scale/zoom animation — grow or shrink an element using transform: scale(). Combine with opacity for a "pop-in" effect. Use for modal appearances, button clicks (subtle 0.95 scale on press), or drawing attention to new elements. Keep scaling subtle in UI (0.95-1.05).

Create a slide/translate animation — move an element from one position to another using transform: translateX/Y(). Use for drawer menus, page transitions, carousel slides, or notifications sliding in from edges. Pair with opacity for natural appearance.

patterns.name: Stagger / Cascade

prompts.textual: Create a stagger/cascade animation — apply the same animation to multiple elements with incrementally delayed start times (e.g., 50-100ms apart). Creates a wave/waterfall effect. Use for list items appearing, card grids loading, or menu items revealing. Adds polish and visual flow.

patterns.name: Rotate / Spin

prompts.textual: Create a rotation/spin animation — continuously rotate an element using transform: rotate(). Use linear timing for constant speed. Primary use: loading spinners. Can also use partial rotation for icon transitions (hamburger → X), or slow rotation for decorative elements.

patterns.name: Loading Dots

prompts.textual: Create a loading dots animation — three dots that pulse/bounce sequentially with staggered delays. The classic "someone is typing" indicator from chat apps. Each dot scales up and down with a slight delay between them. Use for loading states, typing indicators, or processing feedback.

Create a skeleton shimmer animation — a moving highlight gradient that sweeps across a placeholder element, suggesting content is loading. The highlight moves left-to-right continuously. Use a lighter gradient band (40% width) sweeping across the neutral placeholder color. Industry-standard loading pattern.

patterns.name: Marquee / Ticker
patterns.preview_html: Breaking news: design vocabulary is expanding •

prompts.textual: Create a marquee/ticker animation — text or content that continuously scrolls horizontally. Use for news tickers, logo carousels, or announcement banners. Use CSS animation with translateX from 100% to -100%. Modern variant: infinite scrolling logo strip for client showcases.
prompts.html: Breaking news: design vocabulary is expanding •

prompts.textual: Design page/route transitions — the animation between views when navigating. Options: crossfade (overlap opacity), slide (push left/right), scale (shrink out, grow in), shared element (morph common elements). Duration: 200-400ms. Use View Transitions API for native browser support. Adds polish and spatial continuity.
prompts.html: →

patterns.name: Falling / Rain

prompts.textual: Create a falling/rain animation — vertical streaks or particles falling downward at varying speeds and delays. Each particle starts above the container, falls linearly, and fades out at the bottom. Randomize horizontal position, speed, and delay for natural feel. Use for rain effects, snow (slower, with sway), or digital matrix-style falling characters.

patterns.name: Snowfall

prompts.textual: Create a snowfall animation — soft, round particles drifting downward slowly with gentle horizontal sway. Use ease-in timing for gradual acceleration. Vary size (2-6px), opacity, speed, and delay. Add translateX oscillation for wind drift. Larger flakes fall slower (depth illusion). Use for winter themes, holiday promotions, or serene atmospheric effects.

patterns.name: Floating Upward / Bubbles

prompts.textual: Create a floating upward / bubble animation — circles that rise from bottom to top with slight horizontal sway and size growth. Use ease-out for deceleration (bubbles slow as they rise). Add a border instead of fill for translucent bubble look. Use for underwater themes, champagne effects, or peaceful ambient backgrounds.

Create a swinging/pendulum animation — an element rotates back and forth around a fixed pivot point (transform-origin: top center). Use ease-in-out for natural pendulum physics (slows at extremes, fast at center). Use for hanging signs, clock pendulums, or playful dangling elements.

patterns.name: Jello / Wobble

prompts.textual: Create a jello/wobble animation — the element deforms with alternating skew transforms that decrease in amplitude, like jello being poked. Use skewX and skewY together. The oscillation dampens naturally. Use for attention-grabbing buttons, error shake variations, or playful hover effects.

Create a shake/vibrate animation — rapid horizontal oscillation that signals an error or invalid action. Use short duration (300-500ms), play once on trigger. Alternate translateX between -3px and 3px with 5-7 keyframes. Use for invalid form submission, wrong password, or any rejected action feedback.
prompts.html: Error!

patterns.name: Expand / Collapse
patterns.preview_html:
Section
Hidden content that reveals smoothly

prompts.textual: Create an expand/collapse animation — content smoothly reveals or hides by animating max-height (or using CSS grid row transition). Combine with opacity fade for polish. Duration: 200-400ms with ease timing. Use for accordions, dropdown menus, or any toggle-to-reveal pattern.
prompts.html:
Section
Hidden content that reveals smoothly

prompts.textual: Create an orbit/revolve animation — a small element travels in a circular path around a central object. Use CSS rotation on a container with the orbiting element positioned at its edge. The container rotates while the child maintains its orientation (counter-rotate if needed). Use for solar system graphics, loading indicators, or atomic/science themes.

Create a cursor blink animation — a solid block or line that toggles between visible and hidden using step-end timing (not smooth fade). The classic terminal/text editor cursor. Use for code-themed designs, CLI interfaces, or typing indicators. Combine with typewriter text reveal for full effect.
prompts.html: command

prompts.textual: Create a flip card animation — a card with front and back faces that rotates 180deg around the Y-axis on hover/click. Use transform-style: preserve-3d on the container and backface-visibility: hidden on each face. Set perspective on the parent. Use for flashcards, team member bios (photo front, info back), or product feature reveals.
prompts.html:
Front
Back

prompts.textual: Create a pulsing glow animation — an element that rhythmically expands its glow/shadow and slightly scales up, then contracts. Like a heartbeat or breathing. Use for notifications, live status indicators, or drawing attention to important elements. Smooth ease-in-out timing with 2-3 second cycle.

patterns.name: Scatter / Explode

prompts.textual: Create a scatter/explode animation — elements burst outward from a central point in all directions, fading and shrinking as they travel. Use for like/heart explosions, delete confirmations, or celebratory particle bursts. Each particle gets a different angle via translateX/Y. Ease-out timing for deceleration.

patterns.name: Wave / Mexican Wave

prompts.textual: Create a wave/equalizer bar animation — a row of bars that rise and fall sequentially with staggered delays, creating a wave that travels through them. Use scaleY with transform-origin: bottom. Classic for audio visualizers, loading states, and music-themed designs.

patterns.name: Typewriter Reveal
patterns.preview_html: Design Vocabulary

prompts.textual: Create a typewriter reveal animation — text width expands character by character using steps() timing function (one step per character). Combine with a blinking cursor at the right edge. The text appears to be typed in real-time. Use for hero headlines, terminal themes, or AI chat interfaces showing generation.
prompts.html: Design Vocabulary

prompts.textual: Design scroll‑linked motion — animations whose progress is driven by scroll position instead of time. Use scroll‑timeline / View Transitions API where supported or libraries like GSAP ScrollTrigger. Great for pinning sections, parallax heroes, step‑through explainers, and progress‑tied illustrations that move as the user scrolls.
prompts.html: scroll ↕

prompts.textual: Design micro‑interactions for hover/focus — tiny, fast motions like 1–2px lifts, icon nudges, color shifts, or subtle scale changes that confirm interactivity. Duration 120–200ms, ease‑out. Use on buttons, cards, and icons to add polish without feeling “animated for animation’s sake.”

Create scroll‑reveal animations — elements that fade/slide in when they enter the viewport (via IntersectionObserver or scroll‑linked timelines). Use small offsets (8–24px) and 150–250ms durations. Great for section intros, cards in a grid, or stat counters appearing as users scroll down a long page.

categories.name: Image Treatments
categories.description: Photo editing techniques and visual styles applied to images.

prompts.textual: Apply a duotone treatment to an image: convert to grayscale, then map shadows to one color and highlights to another (e.g., purple shadows, pink highlights). Creates bold, branded imagery. Achievable via CSS mix-blend-mode or Photoshop gradient map.

Apply a halftone effect to an image — convert continuous tones into patterns of dots that vary in size. Larger dots = darker areas. Creates a retro print/comic-book look. Use for pop-art style, newspaper aesthetics, or creative editorial imagery.

Apply a clipping mask — crop an image to a non-rectangular shape (circle, hexagon, blob, text). Use CSS clip-path, SVG clipPath, or Figma's mask feature. Creates visually interesting image presentations. Popular for team photos in hexagons, hero images in blobs.

Apply color grading — shift the overall color mood of an image. Warm grading (orange/amber shadows), cool grading (blue shadows), cinematic grading (teal and orange). Use CSS filters or Photoshop. Creates consistent visual mood across all imagery in a brand.

Create a parallax depth effect — layer foreground, midground, and background elements that move at different speeds during scrolling. Creates an immersive, 2.5D depth illusion. Use for landing page heroes, storytelling sites, or creative portfolios.

Create a cutout/knockout image — remove the background from a photo, leaving only the subject. Add a subtle shadow underneath for grounding. Use for product shots, team photos, or any image that needs to float over a custom background. Use AI background removal or manual masking.

Apply a gradient overlay on a photo — layer a semi-transparent gradient (50-70% opacity) over an image to create a branded, colorized look. Ensures text readability on varied image backgrounds. Use for hero sections, card thumbnails, or team member photos with brand-colored overlays.

Apply generous border-radius (12-24px) to images for a modern, friendly feel. Different from circular avatars — maintains the rectangular aspect while softening corners. Increasingly popular in modern UI. Squircle (superellipse) shapes provide even smoother corners than standard border-radius.

Create image tiling — repeating an image or graphic across a surface using background-repeat. Use for brand pattern backgrounds, texture fills, or wallpaper effects. Ensure seamless tiling edges where the pattern repeats. Adjust background-size to control tile dimensions.

Create a split/sliced image — an image divided into vertical or horizontal strips with gaps between them. Creates a dynamic, fragmented look. Use CSS clip-path or multiple background-position values. Great for hero sections, portfolio showcases, or creative transitions.

Apply CSS blend modes to layer effects on photos: multiply (darken), screen (lighten), overlay (contrast boost), color (recolor), luminosity (use photo's lightness with overlay's color). Use mix-blend-mode on an overlaying element. Creates rich, non-destructive visual effects.

Create a before/after image comparison slider — two images overlaid with a draggable divider line. User drags the handle to reveal more of either image. Use for photo editing showcases, renovation before/after, or product comparison. Implement with clip-path or overflow hidden on the top layer.

categories.name: Spacing & Sizing
categories.description: Systematic approaches to whitespace, padding, and proportional sizing.

prompts.textual: Use a 4px base spacing system — all spacing values are multiples of 4 (4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 32, 40, 48, 64px). Creates visual rhythm and consistency. Define as CSS custom properties or design tokens. The foundation of systematic UI design.
prompts.html:
4
8
12
16
24

prompts.textual: Define consistent padding — the internal spacing between an element's border and its content. Use your spacing scale (e.g., 8px compact, 12px default, 16px comfortable, 24px spacious). Consistent padding makes elements feel cohesive and well-crafted.

Use consistent margin — the outer spacing between elements. Use gap in Flexbox/Grid instead of margin where possible. Follow your spacing scale. Vertical margins between sections should be larger than margins between related items within sections.

Define aspect ratios for consistent proportions: 1:1 (square, avatars), 16:9 (widescreen, video), 4:3 (classic), 3:2 (photography), 9:16 (stories/mobile). Use CSS aspect-ratio property. Consistent ratios create visual harmony across cards, thumbnails, and media.
prompts.html:
1:1
16:9
3:4

prompts.textual: Define a modular type scale — font sizes based on a consistent ratio (1.25 Major Third, 1.333 Perfect Fourth, 1.5 Perfect Fifth, 1.618 Golden Ratio). Each size step multiplies by the ratio. Creates harmonious, proportional typography. Example at 1.25: 14, 17.5, 21.9, 27.3, 34.2px.
prompts.html:
32px
24px
18px
14px

patterns.name: Max Width / Measure
patterns.preview_html: Optimal line length is 45-75 characters for comfortable reading.

prompts.textual: Set optimal line length (measure) for body text — max-width: 65ch (65 characters) is the sweet spot. Too wide (90+ chars) = eyes lose track of lines. Too narrow (30 chars) = too many line breaks. Use ch units for responsive measure. Critical for readability in any text-heavy layout.
prompts.html: Optimal line length is 45-75 characters for comfortable reading.

prompts.textual: Use responsive/fluid spacing — spacing values that scale smoothly with viewport width using clamp(). Example: padding: clamp(16px, 4vw, 64px). Spacing grows proportionally on larger screens without breakpoint jumps. Apply to section padding, grid gaps, and margins for smooth scaling.
prompts.html: gap: clamp(8px, 2vw, 32px)

patterns.name: Optical Alignment

prompts.textual: Apply optical alignment — adjust positions visually rather than mathematically. Circles and triangles need slight size increases to appear equal to squares. Play button triangles need right-shift within circular bounds. Icons of different shapes need optical centering, not geometric centering. Trust the eye over the pixel.

patterns.name: Dense vs Comfortable
patterns.preview_html:
Dense
Mode
Layout
Comfort
Mode

prompts.textual: Design density variants — compact (less padding, tighter spacing for power users/data-heavy views) vs comfortable (more padding, generous spacing for casual use). Gmail, Google Sheets offer this toggle. Use 4px padding in dense, 8-12px in default, 16px in comfortable. Let users choose their preference.
prompts.html:

Comfort

categories.name: Accessibility & Contrast
categories.description: Visual design considerations that ensure inclusive, usable interfaces for everyone.

prompts.textual: Ensure high contrast between text and background — WCAG AAA requires 7:1 ratio for normal text, 4.5:1 for large text. Black on white = 21:1 (maximum). Use contrast checker tools. Critical for readability, especially for low-vision users.
prompts.html: 21:1

prompts.textual: Design clear focus indicators — visible outlines that appear when elements are focused via keyboard navigation. Use a high-contrast color, 2px+ width, and 2-3px offset. Never remove focus outlines (outline: none) without providing a custom alternative. Essential for keyboard accessibility.
prompts.html: Button

prompts.textual: Design with color blindness in mind — never use color alone to convey information. Pair colors with icons, labels, or patterns. Avoid red-green only distinctions. Use blue/orange instead of red/green for status. Test with color blindness simulators (Sim Daltonism, Coblis).

patterns.name: Reduced Motion
patterns.preview_html: Reduced motion

prompts.textual: Respect prefers-reduced-motion media query — provide alternatives for users who experience motion sickness or vestibular disorders. Replace animations with simple fades or instant transitions. Keep essential motion (progress indicators), remove decorative motion (parallax, bouncing).
prompts.html: Reduced motion

prompts.textual: Add a "Skip to content" link — a hidden link that appears on keyboard focus, allowing users to bypass navigation and jump directly to main content. Position offscreen by default, bring onscreen on :focus. Essential for keyboard/screen-reader accessibility. First focusable element on the page.
prompts.html: Skip to content

patterns.name: Alt Text / Image Description
patterns.preview_html: alt="..."

prompts.textual: Write meaningful alt text for images — describe the content and function, not just "image of." Decorative images: alt="". Informational: describe what's shown and why it matters. Charts: describe the data trend. Logos: alt="Company Name logo." Alt text is critical for screen readers and SEO.
prompts.html: alt="..."

prompts.textual: Use ARIA labels (aria-label, aria-labelledby, aria-describedby) to provide accessible names for interactive elements that lack visible text labels. Icon-only buttons need aria-label. Complex widgets need aria-describedby for instructions. Don't duplicate visible text — use aria-label only when no visible label exists.
prompts.html: aria-label="Close"

prompts.textual: Ensure minimum touch target size — WCAG 2.5.5 requires at least 44×44px for interactive elements on touch devices. Small icons can have larger invisible hit areas via padding. Spacing between adjacent targets should prevent accidental taps. Apple recommends 44pt, Material Design recommends 48dp.
prompts.html: 44×44px min

prompts.textual: Use semantic color consistently — green/teal for success, amber/yellow for warnings, red for errors/danger, blue for informational. Never use these colors for non-semantic purposes (don't use red for a non-error button). Pair color with icons and text so color-blind users get the same information.
prompts.html:
Success
Warning
Error

prompts.textual: Always use visible, persistent labels for form inputs — not just placeholders. Placeholders disappear when typing, leaving users confused about what field they're filling. Labels above inputs are most accessible. Placeholder text should show example format, not the field name. WCAG 3.3.2 requires labels.
prompts.html:
Email address
user@email.com

prompts.textual: Design error prevention patterns — confirmation dialogs for destructive actions, undo options (Gmail-style), and clear warning messages. Make the safe option (Cancel) more prominent than the destructive one. Use red only for the destructive button. Always allow users to recover from mistakes.
prompts.html:
Delete item?
Cancel
Delete

categories.description: Techniques that simulate three-dimensional space and depth on a 2D canvas.

prompts.textual: Create an isometric cube with three visible faces shaded at different opacities — top lightest, right medium, left darkest. Use 30° angles. Foundation of isometric illustration style used for tech landing pages, infographics, and game art.

prompts.textual: Apply CSS 3D transforms — use perspective() with rotateX/rotateY to tilt elements in 3D space. Creates depth and dynamism. Use for card hover effects, product showcases, or interactive 3D carousels. Set perspective on the parent container (800-1200px).

Create extruded/3D text by stacking multiple text-shadows at 1px increments in progressively darker shades. Creates a block-like depth effect. Use for bold headlines, game titles, retro poster designs, or hero text that needs dramatic impact.
prompts.html: 3D

prompts.textual: Create a paper fold effect — split an element along a crease line with different shading on each half to simulate a folded surface. Use CSS gradients or transforms with rotateY. Great for revealing hidden content, promotional banners, or creative card interactions.

Create a floating layer stack — multiple cards offset diagonally behind each other, creating a deck/pile effect. Each layer slightly darker and offset by 4-8px. Use for feature showcases, pricing plans, or to imply "more content available."

Create a sphere using CSS radial gradient — position the highlight (lighter color) at the top-left to simulate a light source. Darken toward bottom-right. Add a subtle shadow below. Use for 3D decorative elements, planet/globe graphics, or abstract hero visuals.

Create a cylinder shape using CSS — an elliptical top cap, a rectangular body with a horizontal gradient for curvature shading, and an elliptical bottom. Use for chart elements (3D bar charts), product mock-ups, or pillars in illustrations.

Create a 3D torus/donut shape — a ring with shading that suggests three-dimensional volume. Use inner shadows for the concave inner surface and outer shadows for elevation. Popular in abstract 3D illustration trends. Use for decorative hero elements or abstract brand visuals.

Create a 3D ribbon/twisted band — a flowing, ribbon-like shape with visible front and back faces in different colors/shades. Creates a sense of depth and movement. Use for abstract hero graphics, award imagery, or dynamic brand elements.

prompts.textual: Create a levitating/floating object effect — an element that gently bobs up and down with a shadow below that shrinks/expands in sync. The shadow gets smaller and lighter as the object rises, larger and darker as it descends. Use for product showcases, hero CTAs, or app screenshots.

categories.name: Cursors & Micro Indicators
categories.description: Small visual cues that communicate state, status, and interactivity.

prompts.textual: Add a small status dot indicator — a colored circle (8-12px) positioned at the corner of an element. Green = online/active, red = alert, amber = away, gray = offline. Add a border matching the background for visual separation. Use for avatars, icons, or menu items.

Add a notification badge count — a small pill/circle containing a number, positioned at the top-right of an icon or button. Use red/pink for urgency. Show "99+" for large numbers. Use for unread messages, cart items, or pending actions.
prompts.html: 3

prompts.textual: Create a spinner/loading indicator — a circle with one colored arc segment that rotates continuously. Use 2-3px border with only one side colored. Keep size proportional to context (16px inline, 24px button, 40px full-page). Always provide accessible loading text.

Create a pulse/ping animation — a dot that emits expanding, fading rings outward. Use box-shadow animation or a separate scaling element. Signals "live" or "recording" status. Use for live indicators, recording buttons, or attention-drawing dots.

patterns.name: Cursor Pointer Styles

prompts.textual: Design custom cursor styles: default arrow, pointer (hand for links), grab (open hand for draggable), grabbing (closed hand), crosshair (precise selection), text (I-beam), not-allowed (circle with line), wait (hourglass), zoom-in/out. Use CSS cursor property with custom SVG/PNG for branded cursors.

patterns.name: Caret / Chevron

prompts.textual: Use caret/chevron indicators — small V-shaped arrows that signal direction or expandability. Down chevron = dropdown/accordion. Right chevron = navigate forward / drill-in. Animate rotation (0° → 180°) when toggling open/closed states.

Create a vertical ellipsis (kebab) menu — three dots stacked vertically indicating "more options." Horizontal variant = meatball menu. Use for overflow actions on cards, table rows, or list items. Opens a dropdown/popover with additional actions.

Create a hamburger menu icon — three horizontal lines of equal width. The universal symbol for a hidden navigation menu. Animate to an X (close) when menu opens — middle line fades, top/bottom rotate ±45°. Use for mobile navigation or compact sidebars.

Create a step indicator — numbered circles connected by lines showing progress through a multi-step process. Completed steps filled with brand color, current step highlighted, upcoming steps gray. Use for checkout flows, onboarding wizards, or form sequences.

prompts.textual: Design a custom scrollbar — thin track with a rounded thumb. Use ::-webkit-scrollbar CSS pseudo-elements. Modern style: 6-8px wide, transparent track, semi-transparent thumb that darkens on hover. Use for sidebar content, code editors, or custom scroll containers.

categories.name: Data Visualization
categories.description: Chart types and visual encodings for representing data graphically.

prompts.textual: Create a bar chart with vertical columns of varying heights. Use rounded top corners, consistent spacing, and highlight the maximum value in an accent color. Add a subtle horizontal baseline. Use for comparing discrete categories or showing distribution.

patterns.name: Line Chart
patterns.preview_html: <svg width="80" height="44" viewBox="0 0 80 44"><path d="M4,36 L16,28 L28,32 L40,14 L52,20 L64,8 L76,16" fill="none" stroke="#5cfcb6" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round" stroke-linejoin="round"/><path d="M4,36 L16,28 L28,32 L40,14 L52,20 L64,8 L76,16 L76,44 L4,44 Z" fill="url(#lineGrad)" opacity="0.2"/><defs><linearGradient id="lineGrad" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1"><stop offset="0%" stop-color="#5cfcb6"/><stop offset="100%" stop-color="transparent"/></linearGradient></defs></svg>

prompts.textual: Create a line chart with a smooth or segmented line showing trend over time. Add an area fill below the line (gradient fading to transparent). Use for time-series data, stock prices, analytics dashboards, or progress tracking.

prompts.textual: Create a sparkline — a tiny, inline chart with no axes or labels. Shows trend at a glance. Use in table cells, dashboard KPIs, or alongside metrics. Typically 60-100px wide, 16-24px tall. Just the data line, nothing else.

prompts.textual: Create a heatmap — a grid of cells colored by intensity value. Low values = light/transparent, high values = saturated/opaque. Use for GitHub-style contribution graphs, correlation matrices, or geographical density maps.

prompts.textual: Create a scatter plot — individual data points plotted on X/Y axes. Use circle size for a third dimension (bubble chart). Color-code groups. Use for correlation analysis, clustering visualization, or showing relationships between two variables.

prompts.textual: Create a treemap — nested rectangles where size represents value and color represents category. Larger rectangles dominate the space. Use for showing proportional data: disk usage, market cap, budget allocation, or hierarchical data structures.

Create a funnel chart — progressively narrowing horizontal bars showing conversion/dropout at each stage. Widest at top (total leads), narrowest at bottom (conversions). Use for sales pipelines, onboarding flows, or any process where volume decreases at each step.

prompts.textual: Create a Sankey diagram — flowing bands connecting source nodes to target nodes, where band width represents quantity. Shows how values flow and split across stages. Use for energy flow, user journey mapping, budget allocation, or network traffic visualization.

prompts.textual: Create a candlestick chart — each candle shows open, high, low, close values. Green/teal body = close > open (bullish). Red body = close < open (bearish). Thin wicks extend above/below showing high/low. Standard for financial/stock data visualization.

Create a timeline visualization — events plotted chronologically along a horizontal or vertical line. Each event is a dot/node with a card containing date and description. Use for company history, project milestones, changelog, or storytelling. Alternate cards left/right for vertical timelines.

categories.name: Surface Treatments
categories.description: Material simulations, textures, and surface finishes that add realism or tactility.

prompts.textual: Create a metallic/chrome surface using a multi-stop vertical gradient alternating between light and dark grays. Simulates reflective metal. Use for luxury product shots, tech branding, automotive themes, or premium UI accents.

Create a gold foil effect — gradient with warm golds, bright highlights, and dark amber shadows. Use linear-gradient with 5+ stops alternating bright and dark gold. Conveys luxury, premium quality, and celebration. Use for logos, text accents, or premium badges.

Create a holographic/iridescent effect — a rainbow gradient that shifts colors smoothly across the spectrum. Use a wide linear gradient with 6+ hue stops. Animate background-position for a shimmering effect. Use for trend-forward branding, packaging mockups, or premium card designs.

patterns.name: Frosted / Matte

prompts.textual: Create a frosted/matte surface finish — a subtle noise texture over a flat color. Reduces glossiness and adds tactile quality. Use for premium card backgrounds, muted UI surfaces, or simulating matte paper. Apply SVG noise filter at very low opacity (5-10%).

Create a glass refraction effect — transparent element with internal light reflections, edge highlights, and subtle rainbow color splitting. Use layered borders, gradients, and backdrop-filter. More extreme than glassmorphism — simulate actual glass physics.

Create a wood grain texture using repeating linear gradients with varying brown tones. Use for organic/natural design themes, craft branding, furniture product pages, or rustic aesthetics. Can also use an actual wood texture image.

patterns.name: Marble

prompts.textual: Create a marble texture — light stone base with organic veined patterns. Use SVG turbulence filter or a real marble image. Conveys luxury, permanence, and sophistication. Use for high-end product backgrounds, luxury brand collateral, or elegant card surfaces.

Create a fabric/linen texture using a fine cross-hatch of thin semi-transparent lines. Creates the appearance of a woven textile. Use for organic brands, artisanal products, or adding warmth and tactility to digital surfaces.

patterns.name: Paper / Parchment

prompts.textual: Create a paper/parchment texture — off-white base with subtle noise and warm inner shadow. For aged parchment, add sepia tinting and darker edges. Use for document-themed designs, certificates, literary/editorial layouts, or recipe cards.

patterns.name: Concrete / Stone

prompts.textual: Create a concrete/stone texture — medium gray base with heavy grain noise. Rough, industrial feel. Use for brutalist design themes, construction/architecture brands, or urban aesthetics. Pair with bold sans-serif typography.

Create a gradient glass surface — combine glassmorphism (backdrop blur, transparency) with a subtle color gradient overlay. Creates a tinted, frosted glass look. Use for navigation bars, feature cards, or floating panels on vibrant backgrounds. More visually rich than plain glassmorphism.

Create a brushed metal texture — directional fine lines over a metallic gradient, simulating machined aluminum. Use repeating-linear-gradient with hairline alternating light/dark stripes over a base metallic gradient. Use for tech products, Apple-inspired interfaces, or premium UI elements.

patterns.name: Anodized Titanium

prompts.textual: Create an anodized titanium surface — a dark, warm-toned gray with a very fine, subtle grain and soft, diffused highlights. Emulates high-end consumer electronics (like recent smartphones). Use a dark gradient base with low-opacity noise and subtle inner shadows.

Create a soft-touch rubber/silicone surface — extremely matte, light-absorbing dark surface with very soft, broad, and dim highlights. No sharp reflections. Emulates premium tech accessories or grips. Achieved with dark colors, heavy inner shadows, and blurred, low-opacity top highlights.

Create a glossy plastic/acrylic surface — highly reflective, vibrant color with sharp, hard-edged specular highlights. Emulates polished resin or 2000s-era tech. Key is the distinct, bright white highlight shape at the top edge, contrasting with deep shadows at the bottom.

Create a carbon fiber texture — a dark, repeating diagonal woven pattern. Emulates high-performance automotive or aerospace materials. Achieved using complex, layered repeating linear gradients in dark grays and blacks. Conveys speed, strength, and modern engineering.

Create an iridescent foil/pearl finish — soft, pastel rainbow gradients that simulate the inside of an oyster shell or a soap bubble. Very subtle, high-key colors with a soft, glowing sheen. Use for elegant, ethereal, or beauty-focused designs.

patterns.name: Denim / Canvas

prompts.textual: Create a denim/canvas texture — a tight, diagonal cross-hatch pattern over a blue or rugged color base. Simulates durable woven fabric. Use for apparel brands, rugged outdoor themes, or casual, tactile interfaces.

patterns.name: Terrazzo

prompts.textual: Create a terrazzo surface — a composite material pattern featuring scattered, irregular chips of marble, quartz, or glass set in concrete. Use a light background with scattered, colorful, sharp-edged polygons. Trendy in interior design and modern chic branding.

categories.name: Layout Components
categories.description: Structural containers and page-level patterns for organizing content.

prompts.textual: Design a hero section — the large, prominent area at the top of a landing page. Include: headline, subheadline, CTA button, and optional hero image/illustration. Use generous padding, large typography, and a gradient or image background. This is the first thing visitors see.

Design a sidebar navigation — a vertical panel (200-280px wide) anchored to the left or right edge. Contains navigation links, user info, or filters. Use a slightly different background shade from the main content. Collapsible on mobile. Include active state highlighting.

Design a modal/dialog — a centered overlay window on a dimmed backdrop. Include a header with title and close button, body content, and footer with action buttons. Use backdrop-filter for the overlay. Add enter/exit animations. Trap focus within for accessibility.

Design a split-screen layout — the viewport divided into two equal (or proportional) halves. One side for imagery/visual, the other for text/content. Use for comparison pages, login screens (image + form), or product showcases. Creates bold, decisive compositions.

Create a masonry grid layout — items of varying heights arranged in columns, filling vertical space without gaps (like Pinterest). Each column flows independently. Use CSS columns, Masonry.js, or CSS Grid with masonry value. Best for image galleries, portfolios, and inspiration boards.

Design a sticky header — a navigation bar that stays fixed at the top while scrolling. Use position: sticky or fixed. Add backdrop-filter blur and semi-transparent background so content shows through. Often shrinks height on scroll. Include logo, nav links, and CTA.

Design a page footer — the bottom section containing sitemap links organized in columns, legal text, social media icons, newsletter signup, and copyright notice. Use a darker background shade. The footer is a trust signal — include contact info, certifications, and important links.

Design a floating action button (FAB) — a prominent circular button (48-56px) fixed to the bottom-right corner of the screen. Contains a single primary action icon (usually +). Elevated with a strong shadow. Can expand into a speed dial with multiple options. Material Design pattern.
prompts.html: +

prompts.textual: Design a carousel/slider — horizontally scrolling content with navigation dots or arrows. Current slide indicator uses a wider pill shape vs inactive dots. Include prev/next arrow buttons on hover. Use for testimonials, product images, feature showcases, or hero banners.

prompts.textual: Design an accordion component — stacked sections that expand/collapse to reveal content. Only one section open at a time (or allow multiple). Use a chevron that rotates on toggle. Smooth height animation with overflow hidden. Use for FAQs, settings panels, or nested navigation.

prompts.textual: Design a bottom sheet — a panel that slides up from the bottom of the screen. Has a drag handle (small pill) at the top. Can be half-height or full-height, swipeable. Common on mobile for actions, filters, or detail views. Use snap points for intermediate heights.

Each action shows name, keyboard shortcut, and optional icon. Popularized by VS Code, Linear, Raycast. The power-user's favorite navigation pattern.

Include "Mark all as read" action. Group by time (Today, Yesterday). Use for activity feeds, alerts, or system messages.

categories.description: Artistic approaches and visual languages for custom illustrations and graphics.

prompts.textual: Create a flat illustration — simple geometric shapes, solid colors, no gradients or shadows. Minimal detail, bold shapes, clean edges. The dominant style for SaaS websites, tech products, and modern apps. Think Slack, Dropbox, or Mailchimp illustration style.

prompts.textual: Create an abstract gradient illustration — fluid, organic shapes with rich color gradients and blur effects. No defined objects, just mood and atmosphere. Use for app backgrounds, music-related design, or conceptual hero sections. Think Apple keynote backdrops.
