prompts.textual: Create a paper cutout/collage illustration — flat shapes that appear to be cut from colored paper, layered with subtle shadows between layers. Each piece has a slight shadow suggesting physical depth. Use for editorial design, children's books, or craft-inspired branding.

Create a glassmorphic illustration — 3D-looking glass cards and shapes floating in space with frosted transparency. Layered translucent panels with colored light bleeding between them. Used for fintech, dashboard art, and futuristic UI concept illustrations.

Create a retro/vintage illustration style — aged color palette (muted yellows, browns, reds), halftone textures, double-line borders, ornamental frames, and period-appropriate typography. Simulate worn/aged printing. Use for craft beer, barbershops, heritage brands, or nostalgic campaigns.

prompts.textual: Create stipple/pointillism art — images formed entirely from small dots of varying density. Denser dots = darker areas, sparser = lighter. No lines or fills — just dots. Use for editorial illustration, scientific diagrams, tattoo-style graphics, or sophisticated monochrome art.

prompts.textual: Create Bauhaus-style illustration — primary colors (red, blue, yellow) with black, geometric shapes (circle, square, triangle), asymmetric composition, and sans-serif typography. Inspired by the 1920s German design school. Use for art-forward branding, poster design, or modernist-influenced web design.

Create noir/chiaroscuro-style illustration — dramatic contrast between light and dark with minimal mid-tones. Strong directional lighting creates deep shadows and bright highlights. Use for film noir themes, dramatic storytelling, mystery/thriller brands, or sophisticated monochrome photography treatments.

Create a 3D clay illustration — objects rendered as if made from soft, matte modeling clay. Soft lighting, rounded edges, no sharp corners, pastel or bright colors. Very popular in modern UI, onboarding screens, and friendly tech products. Often created in Spline, Blender, or Cinema4D.

patterns.name: Ukiyo-e / Woodblock

prompts.textual: Create an Ukiyo-e/woodblock style illustration — inspired by traditional Japanese prints. Use bold, flowing outlines, flat muted colors (indigo, vermilion, pale yellow), stylized waves/clouds, and a subtle paper texture. Great for cultural themes, tea brands, or artistic posters.

Create a Risograph illustration — simulates the Japanese stencil duplicator printing process. Use 2-3 bright, highly saturated spot colors (like neon pink and cyan), multiply blend modes where they overlap, slight misregistration (offset), and heavy grain/halftone texture.

patterns.name: Line & Wash

prompts.textual: Create a line and wash illustration — crisp, loose ink outlines (often black or dark brown) combined with loose, expressive watercolor washes that don't perfectly stay within the lines. Feels organic, artistic, and human. Great for food packaging, travel blogs, or boutique brands.

patterns.name: Vaporwave
patterns.preview_html: A E S T H E T I C

prompts.textual: Create a Vaporwave illustration — surreal combination of 80s/90s corporate design, early 3D computer graphics, classical busts/statues, Japanese text, grid floors, and neon pink/cyan/purple gradients. Highly nostalgic and ironic.
prompts.html: A E S T H E T I C

patterns.name: Anime / Manga

prompts.textual: Create an Anime/Manga style illustration — characterized by cel shading (hard-edged shadows, no smooth gradients), dynamic angles, speed lines, exaggerated expressions, and distinct eye/hair stylization. Use for gaming, youth-oriented brands, or energetic storytelling.

Create 3D soft stylized illustrations — objects rendered with thick, rounded edges, glossy or matte pastel materials, and soft studio lighting. Looks like high-end vinyl toys or inflated balloons. Highly engaging and approachable, often used for modern app icons and hero graphics.

patterns.name: Doodle / Scribble
patterns.preview_html: <svg width="60" height="50" viewBox="0 0 60 50"><path d="M15,25 Q20,15 30,25 T45,25" fill="none" stroke="#1a1a24" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/><path d="M25,15 L25,35 M35,15 L35,35" stroke="#1a1a24" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>

prompts.textual: Create doodle/scribble illustrations — looks like it was drawn quickly with a thick marker or sharpie. Unpolished, overlapping lines, and simple symbolic representations. Conveys authenticity, brainstorming, and human touch.
prompts.html:
<!-- HTML/CSS implementation for Doodle / Scribble -->
<svg width="60" height="50" viewBox="0 0 60 50"><path d="M15,25 Q20,15 30,25 T45,25" fill="none" stroke="#1a1a24" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/><path d="M25,15 L25,35 M35,15 L35,35" stroke="#1a1a24" stroke-width="2" stroke-linecap="round"/></svg>

prompts.textual: Create a collage/mixed media illustration — combine vintage black-and-white photography, bright solid color shapes, torn paper textures, and bold typography. Feels artistic, edgy, and editorial. Great for fashion, music, and culture brands.
prompts.html: ART

categories.name: Transitions & Transforms
categories.description: CSS transform properties and transition effects that modify element appearance.

prompts.textual: Apply a skew transform (transform: skewX(-10deg)) to slant an element diagonally. Creates dynamic, forward-leaning energy. Use for action-oriented designs, speed/motion themes, or breaking out of rigid grid layouts.

Apply rotation (transform: rotate(12deg)) to tilt an element. Small rotations (5-15°) add casual, playful energy. Use for scattered photo layouts, sticker effects, or breaking visual monotony. Full 360° rotation for loading spinners.

Add a scale-on-hover effect — element grows slightly (1.05-1.1) when hovered. Use transform: scale() with a smooth transition (0.2-0.3s ease). The most common hover interaction for cards, buttons, and thumbnails. Subtle but satisfying.

Apply a flip/mirror transform — scaleX(-1) for horizontal mirroring or scaleY(-1) for vertical. Use for creating symmetrical designs from one half, reflection effects, or the classic "card flip" interaction with rotateY(180deg) and backface-visibility: hidden.
prompts.html:
R
R

prompts.textual: Animate clip-path to create shape morphing or directional reveal transitions. Transition between polygon shapes — circle to full (iris reveal), inset for box wipes, or custom polygons. Use for page transitions, image reveals, or creative hover effects.

Create a morphing blob animation — continuously animate border-radius between different organic values to create a shape that appears to breathe and shift. Use 8-value border-radius for asymmetric shapes. Combine with gradient fills for mesmerizing background elements.

patterns.name: Perspective Tilt on Hover

prompts.textual: Add a 3D tilt effect on hover — the card tilts toward the cursor position using perspective and rotateX/rotateY transforms. More dynamic than simple scale. Use JS to track cursor position relative to element center for true cursor-following tilt. Popular for product cards and portfolio items.

Apply elastic/spring easing — the element overshoots its target and oscillates before settling. Use cubic-bezier curves or spring physics (framer-motion, react-spring). More natural and playful than standard easing. Use for modal entrances, drawer opens, or notifications appearing.

patterns.name: Path Motion
patterns.preview_html: <svg width="80" height="40" viewBox="0 0 80 40"><path d="M4,20 Q20,4 40,20 Q60,36 76,20" fill="none" stroke="#2a2a3a" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4"/><circle cx="0" cy="0" r="4" fill="#7c5cfc"><animateMotion dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite" path="M4,20 Q20,4 40,20 Q60,36 76,20"/></circle></svg>

prompts.textual: Animate an element along an SVG path — the element follows a custom bezier curve trajectory. Use CSS offset-path + offset-distance or SVG animateMotion. Use for creative loading animations, flying icons, or visualizing a journey/process with an animated traveler.
prompts.html:
<!-- HTML/CSS implementation for Path Motion -->
<svg width="80" height="40" viewBox="0 0 80 40"><path d="M4,20 Q20,4 40,20 Q60,36 76,20" fill="none" stroke="#2a2a3a" stroke-width="1" stroke-dasharray="4"/><circle cx="0" cy="0" r="4" fill="#7c5cfc"><animateMotion dur="3s" repeatCount="indefinite" path="M4,20 Q20,4 40,20 Q60,36 76,20"/></circle></svg>

prompts.textual: Create a staggered grid entrance animation — grid items appear one by one with incrementing delays, creating a cascade/wave effect across the grid. Delay can follow left-to-right, top-to-bottom, diagonal, or radial patterns. Use for image galleries, dashboard widgets, or portfolio grids loading in.

categories.name: Print & Editorial
categories.description: Design concepts rooted in traditional print, publishing, and editorial layout.

prompts.textual: Create a multi-column text layout — text flowing across 2-4 columns like a newspaper or magazine. Use CSS columns property or CSS Grid. Include proper column gaps (16-24px), and optionally a thin vertical rule between columns. Use for long-form text, articles, or editorial landing pages.

Create a pull quote — a highlighted excerpt from the body text, styled distinctively and pulled out of the text flow. Use larger font size, italic, a colored left border or large quotation marks. Use for breaking up long text, emphasizing key messages, or adding visual interest to articles.
prompts.html:
"Design is
intelligence
made visible"

prompts.textual: Add an image caption — small text below (or overlaying) an image describing its content. Use a smaller font size (12-14px), muted color, and optionally a figure number. Essential for editorial, documentation, and portfolio layouts. Use the <figcaption> HTML element.
prompts.html: Fig. 1 — Caption text here

prompts.textual: Create a full-bleed element — content that extends to the very edge of the viewport/page with no margins. The dashed inner line represents the safe area. Use for hero images, background sections, or immersive photography. In print, bleed extends 3mm past the trim line.

Design a spread layout — a two-page composition designed as a unified whole, like an open book or magazine. The left and right pages work together visually. Images and elements can cross the center spine. Use for lookbooks, portfolios, or immersive digital storytelling.

patterns.name: Orphan & Widow Control
patterns.preview_html: Good typography avoids leaving single words dangling alone on a line at the end of paragraphs.

prompts.textual: Control orphans and widows in typography — an orphan is a single word on the last line of a paragraph; a widow is the last line of a paragraph at the top of a new column/page. Use CSS orphans/widows properties, text-wrap: balance, or manual   to prevent single-word final lines.
prompts.html: Good typography avoids leaving single words dangling alone on a line at the end of paragraphs.

patterns.name: Baseline Grid
patterns.preview_html:
Title
Body text aligned to
the baseline grid

prompts.textual: Align all text to a baseline grid — set a consistent base line-height (e.g., 4px or 8px unit) and ensure all text, headings, and spacing are multiples of this value. Creates a harmonious vertical rhythm throughout the page. Fundamental to professional typographic design.
prompts.html:
Title
Body text aligned to
the baseline grid

prompts.textual: Create a sidebar callout/margin note — a small annotation placed in the margin alongside body text. Use for definitions, cross-references, editorial comments, or supplementary information. Visually distinct from body text (smaller, different color, bordered). A hallmark of thoughtful editorial design.
prompts.html: Note text

patterns.name: Footnote
patterns.preview_html:
Body text with reference1
1 Footnote explanation text.

prompts.textual: Design footnotes — superscript reference numbers in body text linked to explanatory notes at the bottom of the page/section. The footnote area is separated by a short horizontal rule. Use for citations, clarifications, legal disclaimers, or supplementary details without cluttering the main text.
prompts.html:
Body text with reference1
1 Footnote explanation text.

prompts.textual: Design an infographic layout — combine large statistics (hero numbers), icons, mini-charts, and brief explanatory text into a visually compelling data story. Use a clear visual hierarchy: biggest number first, supporting data below. Color-code related data. Design for scanning, not reading.
prompts.html:
73%
of users prefer dark mode

categories.name: Interaction States
categories.description: Visual feedback for different states of interactive elements.

prompts.textual: Design the default/rest state — how an interactive element looks before any interaction. Should clearly communicate that it's interactive (colored fill, underline for links). The baseline from which all other states derive.
prompts.html: Default

prompts.textual: Design the hover state — visual change when the cursor moves over an element. Typically: darken/lighten the background by 5-10%, add a subtle shadow, or change cursor. Use transition: all 0.2s ease. Must be distinct enough to notice but not jarring.
prompts.html: Hover

prompts.textual: Design the active/pressed state — how an element looks during the click/tap. Typically: slightly darker, scale down to 0.95-0.98, reduced shadow (feels pushed into the surface). Brief transition. Provides immediate tactile feedback.
prompts.html: Active

prompts.textual: Design the disabled state — element is visible but non-interactive. Use reduced opacity (40-60%), grayed-out colors, and cursor: not-allowed. Remove hover effects. Communicate that the action is temporarily unavailable. Always provide context for why it's disabled (tooltip).
prompts.html: Disabled

patterns.name: Loading State
patterns.preview_html: Loading

prompts.textual: Design the loading state — element shows it's processing. Replace or supplement text with a spinner, use a progress bar, or add a shimmer effect. Prevent double-clicks by disabling the element. Slightly reduce opacity. Always provide feedback that something is happening.
prompts.html: Loading

prompts.textual: Design the error state — red/danger color (#FC3C3C or similar), red border, error icon, and clear error message below the element. Use for form validation errors, failed submissions, or destructive action warnings. Don't just use color — include an icon and text for accessibility.
prompts.html: Error

prompts.textual: Design the success state — green/teal color with a checkmark icon. Confirms that an action completed successfully. Use for validated form fields, successful submissions, or completed tasks. Combine with a brief success animation (checkmark drawing in).
prompts.html: Success ✓

prompts.textual: Design the selected/active state — the currently chosen item among peers (tab, filter, radio option). Use brand color fill, bold text, or a bottom border/underline. Inactive siblings should be clearly less prominent. The difference between active and inactive must be unambiguous.
prompts.html:
Active
Tab 2

prompts.textual: Design the dragging state — when an element is being dragged via drag-and-drop. Slightly rotated (2-5°), elevated shadow, reduced opacity (70%), and cursor: grabbing. A semi-transparent "ghost" copy follows the cursor while the original shows a drop placeholder.
prompts.html: Dragging

categories.name: Backgrounds & Scenes
categories.description: Full background treatments and atmospheric effects for pages and sections.

prompts.textual: Create a gradient mesh background — multiple large, blurred, semi-transparent colored orbs on a light or dark base. Purple, pink, and teal blobs with 40-80px blur. Creates an ethereal, ambient atmosphere. Animate with slow floating motion. Dominant background trend in modern SaaS and AI product sites.

Create a starfield/particle background — scattered tiny dots of varying sizes and opacities on a dark or light sky gradient. Add optional twinkling animation. Use for space themes, hero sections, or tech products. Can add thin connecting lines for a constellation/network effect.

patterns.name: Geometric Pattern BG
patterns.preview_html: <svg width="80" height="50" viewBox="0 0 80 50"><rect width="80" height="50" rx="8" fill="#f5f7ff"/><polygon points="0,50 30,20 60,50" fill="rgba(124,92,252,0.10)" stroke="rgba(124,92,252,0.20)" stroke-width="0.5"/><polygon points="20,0 50,30 80,0" fill="rgba(252,92,140,0.06)" stroke="rgba(252,92,140,0.14)" stroke-width="0.5"/><polygon points="60,50 80,20 80,50" fill="rgba(92,252,182,0.07)" stroke="rgba(92,252,182,0.14)" stroke-width="0.5"/></svg>

prompts.textual: Create a geometric pattern background — low-poly triangles, overlapping shapes, or tessellated patterns at very low opacity. Use for tech-forward designs, conference sites, or corporate pages. Keep opacity below 10% so it adds texture without competing with content.
prompts.html:
<!-- HTML/CSS implementation for Geometric Pattern BG -->
<svg width="80" height="50" viewBox="0 0 80 50"><rect width="80" height="50" rx="8" fill="#f5f7ff"/><polygon points="0,50 30,20 60,50" fill="rgba(124,92,252,0.10)" stroke="rgba(124,92,252,0.20)" stroke-width="0.5"/><polygon points="20,0 50,30 80,0" fill="rgba(252,92,140,0.06)" stroke="rgba(252,92,140,0.14)" stroke-width="0.5"/><polygon points="60,50 80,20 80,50" fill="rgba(92,252,182,0.07)" stroke="rgba(92,252,182,0.14)" stroke-width="0.5"/></svg>

patterns.name: Noise Overlay Background

prompts.textual: Add a subtle noise texture overlay to a solid or gradient background. Use SVG feTurbulence at 3-5% opacity. Prevents the "too clean" digital look, adds depth and subtle texture. Works on both light and dark backgrounds. Almost invisible but makes a subconscious difference.

patterns.name: Gradient Wave Divider

prompts.textual: Create a wave-shaped section divider — an SVG curve between two sections of different colors. Use preserveAspectRatio="none" to stretch across the viewport. Creates organic, flowing transitions instead of hard horizontal lines. Popular for landing pages between hero and content sections.

Create a diagonal/angled section divider — a skewed boundary between two sections. Use CSS transform: skewY(-3deg to -6deg) or an SVG diagonal line. Creates dynamic energy and forward momentum. Use for landing pages, pricing sections, or feature areas that need visual drama.

Create a synthwave/retrowave background — sky gradient, perspective grid floor receding to the horizon, neon sun (circle with glow), and optional mountain silhouettes. Use CSS perspective transform on the grid. Captures 1980s sci-fi/arcade nostalgia. Works on both light and dark palettes.

Create a bokeh background — soft, out-of-focus circles of light in various sizes and colors, simulating a camera's shallow depth-of-field. Use semi-transparent circles on a light or dark background. Animate with gentle floating motion. Use for festive themes, photography sites, or dreamy hero backgrounds.

Create layered mountain/hill silhouettes — overlapping wave-like shapes in progressively lighter or darker shades from back to front. Creates depth through atmospheric perspective. Use for nature-themed designs, outdoor brands, or as a decorative footer/section background. Animate layers at different scroll speeds for parallax.
prompts.html:
<!-- HTML/CSS implementation for Layered Mountains -->
<svg width="80" height="50" viewBox="0 0 80 50"><rect width="80" height="50" fill="#e0f2fe"/><path d="M0,45 Q15,30 30,38 Q45,20 60,32 Q75,28 80,35 L80,50 L0,50 Z" fill="#bfdbfe"/><path d="M0,48 Q20,35 40,42 Q55,30 70,38 Q80,34 80,42 L80,50 L0,50 Z" fill="#93c5fd"/><path d="M0,50 Q25,40 50,46 Q65,38 80,44 L80,50 Z" fill="#60a5fa"/></svg>

patterns.name: Animated Particles

prompts.textual: Create animated floating particles — small dots of varying sizes and colors that drift slowly across a dark background. Use CSS animation or a JS particle library (particles.js, tsparticles). Particles can connect with thin lines when near each other. Use for tech/AI themes, hero backgrounds, or cosmic aesthetics.

categories.name: Dark Mode Patterns
categories.description: Specific design techniques and considerations for dark-themed interfaces.

prompts.textual: In dark mode, express elevation through surface lightness — higher-elevation surfaces are slightly lighter, not shadowed. Base surface (dp0) is darkest; each elevation level adds ~2-4% white overlay. Shadows are less visible in dark mode, so lightness differences become the primary depth cue.
prompts.html:
dp0
dp1
dp2
dp4

prompts.textual: In dark mode, desaturate and lighten brand colors — fully saturated colors vibrate against dark backgrounds and cause eye strain. Reduce saturation by 10-20% and increase lightness. Maintain enough contrast (WCAG 4.5:1 min). Pastels work better against dark than against white.

In dark mode, use very subtle borders (white at 5-10% opacity) to define edges between same-shade surfaces. Borders are more important in dark mode because shadows are less visible. Use rgba(255,255,255,0.06-0.12) instead of solid gray colors for more natural blending.

In dark mode, accent elements can emit glow — use colored box-shadow to create light-emission effects impossible on white backgrounds. Colored elements appear to be light sources. Use for active states, selected items, or CTAs. This is a dark-mode superpower.

Create a dark hero gradient — subtle color-shifted gradient on a near-black background. Shift from deep purple-black at top through pure dark to deep blue-black at bottom. Adds richness and prevents flat, dead backgrounds. Keep the color shifts very subtle (barely perceptible).

Prepare an inverted/light version of your logo for dark backgrounds — swap dark fills to light, adjust colors for sufficient contrast on dark surfaces. Some logos need completely different color treatments for dark mode. Use CSS prefers-color-scheme media query or a theme toggle to swap between versions.
prompts.html:
LOGO
LOGO

patterns.name: Dark Illustration
patterns.preview_html: <svg width="60" height="40" viewBox="0 0 60 40"><rect x="8" y="12" width="22" height="20" rx="3" fill="rgba(124,92,252,0.2)" stroke="#7c5cfc" stroke-width="1"/><polygon points="8,12 19,4 30,12" fill="rgba(124,92,252,0.15)" stroke="#7c5cfc" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="15" y="22" width="6" height="10" fill="#fcac5c" opacity="0.4"/><circle cx="48" cy="10" r="5" fill="#fcac5c" opacity="0.3"/></svg>

prompts.textual: Adapt illustrations for dark mode — reduce color saturation and brightness, use semi-transparent fills instead of solids, add subtle strokes for definition. Dark mode illustrations should feel native, not just inverted. Consider separate dark/light illustration sets for the best result.
prompts.html:
<!-- HTML/CSS implementation for Dark Illustration -->
<svg width="60" height="40" viewBox="0 0 60 40"><rect x="8" y="12" width="22" height="20" rx="3" fill="rgba(124,92,252,0.2)" stroke="#7c5cfc" stroke-width="1"/><polygon points="8,12 19,4 30,12" fill="rgba(124,92,252,0.15)" stroke="#7c5cfc" stroke-width="1"/><rect x="15" y="22" width="6" height="10" fill="#fcac5c" opacity="0.4"/><circle cx="48" cy="10" r="5" fill="#fcac5c" opacity="0.3"/></svg>

prompts.textual: Choose between true black (#000000) and dark gray (#121218) for dark mode backgrounds. True black saves battery on OLED screens but creates harsh contrast and "black hole" effect. Dark gray is more comfortable, allows shadow visibility, and supports elevation changes. Most design systems use dark gray (Material: #121212).
prompts.html:
#000
#121218

prompts.textual: Design a dark mode toggle — a switch control with sun/moon icons that transitions between light and dark themes. Animate the toggle with a smooth slide and icon crossfade. Can also use a dropdown for system/light/dark options. Persist the preference in localStorage. Respect prefers-color-scheme for initial state.
prompts.html: 🌙

categories.name: Responsive Design
categories.description: Techniques for adapting designs across screen sizes and devices.

prompts.textual: Design for responsive breakpoints — common breakpoints: 640px (mobile), 768px (tablet), 1024px (laptop), 1280px (desktop), 1536px (wide). Stack columns on mobile, show side-by-side on desktop. Use CSS media queries or container queries. Mobile-first approach: design for small screens first, enhance for larger.
prompts.html:
S
M
L

prompts.textual: Use fluid typography — font sizes that scale smoothly with viewport width using CSS clamp(). Example: font-size: clamp(1rem, 2.5vw + 0.5rem, 3rem). Text grows and shrinks fluidly between min and max values. No jumps at breakpoints. Modern alternative to media query font-size changes.
prompts.html:
Title
clamp(1rem, 4vw, 3rem)

prompts.textual: Use CSS container queries — @container rules that let components respond to their parent container's size instead of the viewport. Components become truly self-contained and reusable. Use for cards, widgets, or any component that might appear in different-width containers.
prompts.html: @container

prompts.textual: Serve responsive images — use srcset and sizes attributes to deliver different image resolutions for different screen sizes/densities. Use the <picture> element for art direction (different crops per breakpoint). Serve WebP/AVIF with JPEG fallback. Critical for performance.
prompts.html:
→
→

prompts.textual: Design mobile-first — start with the smallest screen and progressively enhance for larger viewports. Use min-width media queries (not max-width). The mobile layout is the base CSS; larger screens add complexity. Forces content prioritization and ensures the core experience works everywhere.
prompts.html: 1st

prompts.textual: Design grid collapse behavior — define how multi-column grids restructure at smaller breakpoints. 3 columns → 2 columns → 1 column (stacked). Cards may change from horizontal to vertical layout. Images may go from side-by-side to stacked. Plan the collapse logic for every grid in your design.
prompts.html: →

prompts.textual: Design responsive navigation — full horizontal nav on desktop, collapsing into a hamburger menu on mobile. Define the breakpoint where the switch occurs. Mobile menu can be full-screen overlay, slide-in drawer, or bottom sheet. Ensure all nav items remain accessible on all screen sizes.
prompts.html:
Home
About
→

prompts.textual: Use CSS aspect-ratio for responsive media containers — maintains proportions regardless of width. Use for video embeds (16:9), image thumbnails (4:3), square cards (1:1). Replaces the old padding-top percentage hack. The container scales fluidly while maintaining its aspect ratio.
prompts.html: 16:9 fluid

categories.name: Micro-Interactions
categories.description: Small, focused animations that provide feedback and delight.

prompts.textual: Create a ripple effect on button click — a circular wave that expands from the click point outward, fading as it grows. Material Design signature interaction. Use JS to position the ripple at click coordinates, then CSS animation for the expanding circle at 25% opacity white.
prompts.html: Click

patterns.name: Like Heart Animation

prompts.textual: Create a like/heart animation — heart scales up from 0 to 1.2 then settles at 1.0 with a spring ease, simultaneously changing color from gray to red/pink. Optionally add small particles/confetti bursting outward. Use for social media like buttons, favorite toggles, or appreciation actions.

patterns.name: Checkbox Check Animation

prompts.textual: Create a checkbox check animation — when checked, the box fills with brand color (scale or fade transition), then the checkmark draws in with a stroke-dasharray/stroke-dashoffset animation. Total duration ~300ms. Makes form completion feel rewarding.

Create a hover underline animation — an underline that slides in from left (width: 0 → 100%) or center (scaleX: 0 → 1 with transform-origin: center) on hover. More elegant than text-decoration: underline. Use for navigation links or inline text links. Duration 200-300ms.

prompts.textual: Create a scroll progress indicator — a thin bar at the very top of the page that fills left-to-right as the user scrolls down. Width = (scrollY / (docHeight - viewportHeight)) * 100%. Use position: fixed, top: 0, z-index: 9999. Great for articles, documentation, or long-form content.

Create a number counter animation — a number that counts up from 0 to the target value when it scrolls into view. Use requestAnimationFrame with easing for smooth counting. Apply font-variant-numeric: tabular-nums so digits don't shift width. Use for statistics, metrics, or achievement displays.

prompts.textual: Animate the transition from skeleton placeholder to real content — skeleton blocks have a shimmer animation (moving gradient highlight). When data loads, crossfade from skeleton to content with a 200ms opacity transition. Prevents layout shift by matching skeleton dimensions to real content.

prompts.textual: Adjust kerning (letter-spacing) — the space between specific letter pairs. Tighten tracking for headlines (-0.5 to -1px), loosen for small caps and labels (+2 to +4px). prompts.textual: Adjust leading (line-height) — the vertical space between lines of text. Headlines: 1.1-1.2 (tight). Body text: 1.5-1.7 (comfortable). Small text: 1.6-1.8. Too tight = claustrophobic; too loose = disconnected. Line-height directly controls readability and page rhythm.

prompts.textual: Enable typographic ligatures — special combined glyphs for letter pairs that would otherwise collide (fi, fl, ff, ffi). Use font-feature-settings: 'liga' for standard, 'dlig' for discretionary. Adds polish to editorial typography. Most quality fonts include standard ligatures.

Use CSS hanging-punctuation property or manual negative margins. Marks that "indent" the first line (", ', -) should hang outside the text block for professional typesetting.

categories.name: Psychological Design Principles
categories.description: Visual techniques that leverage human psychology to guide behavior and perception.

prompts.textual: Use visual weight to create hierarchy — larger, darker, more saturated elements carry more visual weight and draw the eye first. Balance a single heavy element with several lighter ones. Place the "heaviest" element at your focal point. Weight comes from: size, color, density, contrast, and isolation.

Apply the Gestalt principle of proximity — elements placed close together are perceived as related/grouped. Use consistent spacing: tighter spacing within groups, wider spacing between groups. No borders needed — space itself creates grouping. Foundation of information architecture in UI.

Apply the Gestalt principle of similarity — elements that share visual properties (color, shape, size) are perceived as belonging to the same group. Use consistent styling for same-category items. Break similarity deliberately to draw attention to exceptions (the Von Restorff effect).

Apply the Von Restorff (isolation) effect — an item that differs from its neighbors is remembered better. Make your primary CTA visually distinct from surrounding elements in color, size, or shape. Use for pricing table highlights ("Most Popular"), key features, or the primary action among a group.

Apply Fitts's Law — the time to reach a target is proportional to the distance and inversely proportional to its size. Make important targets (CTAs, submit buttons) large and position them near likely cursor/finger positions. Minimum touch target: 44×44px (WCAG). Larger targets = faster, easier interaction.

patterns.name: Social Proof Signals
patterns.preview_html: +2.4k users

prompts.textual: Design social proof elements — overlapping avatar stacks with "+N users" count, star ratings, client logos, testimonial cards, or "X people are viewing this." These visual cues leverage social proof psychology. Position near CTAs to increase conversion. Overlapping avatars use negative margins.
prompts.html: +2.4k users

prompts.textual: Apply Hick's Law — decision time increases logarithmically with the number of options. Reduce choices to speed up decisions. Use progressive disclosure (show basics first, details on demand). Limit navigation to 5-7 items. Pricing tables: 3 plans max. Too many CTAs = no action taken.

prompts.textual: Apply Miller's Law — people can hold about 7 (±2) items in working memory. Break content into chunks of 3-5 items. Use visual grouping, whitespace, and headings to create manageable clusters. Phone numbers are chunked (555-123-4567), not (5551234567). Apply to nav menus, feature lists, and form sections.

Use anchoring — the first piece of information (the anchor) influences subsequent judgments. Show the original/higher price before the discounted price. Place the premium plan first in pricing tables so the standard plan feels affordable. The anchor creates a reference frame for all subsequent comparisons.

prompts.textual: Apply the serial position effect — people remember the first (primacy) and last (recency) items in a list best. Place your most important features, nav items, or options at the beginning and end. The middle items are most likely to be forgotten. Put key CTAs first and last in navigation.

categories.name: Trending & Modern Effects
categories.description: Cutting-edge visual styles and effects dominating contemporary design in 2024–2026.

prompts.textual: Create a liquid glass effect (Apple iOS 26 / visionOS style): semi-transparent surface with high backdrop-filter blur (20-40px), increased saturation (saturate 1.5-2.0), a subtle inner white glow along the top edge, very thin white border (20-30% opacity), and a soft specular highlight (elliptical white gradient at the top). The surface should feel like real glass — refracting and tinting the content behind it. Colors from the background should bleed through with a frosted, luminous quality. Add a very subtle inner shadow at the bottom edge. This is more refined than basic glassmorphism — the key difference is the saturation boost, multi-layered specular highlights, and the organic, flowing feel of the reflections.

Create a liquid glass card component: a content card with the full liquid glass treatment — high blur (24px+), saturation boost, layered specular highlights on top half, thin luminous border, and content floating inside. Icons and elements within the card should also have subtle glass treatments. The card should feel like it's floating in space, bending light around it. Use for notification cards, widget panels, control centers, or any elevated UI element.

Create a liquid glass tab bar (iOS 26 style): a pill-shaped floating navigation bar with liquid glass material — high blur, saturation, luminous top edge, and rounded pill shape. Active tab icon is tinted with the accent color and may have a subtle glow. The entire bar floats above content with a soft shadow. This replaces the traditional opaque bottom tab bar with something that feels integrated with the content flowing behind it.

patterns.name: Caustics

prompts.textual: Create caustic light patterns — the shimmering, web-like light patterns you see at the bottom of a swimming pool or through glass. Use SVG turbulence filters with high contrast, or animated overlapping radial gradients in cyan/teal. Animate slowly for a mesmerizing, underwater refraction effect. Use for luxury brands, water-themed design, or ambient backgrounds.

Create a prismatic/rainbow refraction effect — thin rainbow light streaks that appear to refract through a glass or crystal surface. Use blurred, narrow gradient strips at slight angles. Simulate light dispersion (splitting white light into spectrum). Use for premium product photography, jewelry brands, or sci-fi interfaces. Animate with subtle shifting for a living, light-catching feel.

Create a lens flare effect — a bright light source with streaks, secondary ghost circles, and anamorphic horizontal lines. The main light has a bright core with a soft halo. Ghost circles appear at intervals along a line from the source (lens elements catching light). Use for cinematic hero sections, photography themes, or adding dramatic atmosphere.

Create a smoke/fog effect — soft, amorphous cloudy shapes using heavily blurred semi-transparent ellipses. Layer multiple at different sizes, positions, and opacities. Animate with slow drifting motion using CSS transforms. Use for atmospheric backgrounds, mystery/horror themes, or adding depth to dark scenes. Can be colored for neon smoke effects.

prompts.textual: Create a Bento grid layout — a mosaic-style grid where cards of varying sizes (1×1, 2×1, 1×2, 2×2) are arranged like a Japanese bento box. Popularized by Apple's feature pages. Each cell showcases one feature/product with an icon, title, and visual. Use generous border-radius, consistent gaps, and subtle borders. The asymmetric grid creates visual interest while maintaining order.

patterns.name: Grain Film Effect

prompts.textual: Create a film grain effect — a visible noise texture that mimics analog film stock. Use SVG feTurbulence with mix-blend-mode: overlay at 20-40% opacity. More pronounced than subtle noise gradients. Adds cinematic warmth, vintage authenticity, and tactile quality. Animate the seed value for moving grain (like real film). Use for photography portfolios, editorial design, or indie brand aesthetics.

patterns.name: Displacement / Distortion

prompts.textual: Create a displacement/distortion effect — warp an image or element as if viewed through textured glass or water. Use SVG feDisplacementMap with feTurbulence as the displacement source. Adjust scale for intensity. Use for artistic photo treatments, hover effects (distort on mouseover), or creating organic, liquid-like visuals.

Create a moiré pattern — an optical interference pattern created by overlaying two sets of lines or grids at slightly different angles or scales. Creates mesmerizing, vibrating visual effects. Use overlapping repeating-radial-gradient or repeating-linear-gradient at slight offsets. Use for avant-garde design, op-art inspired visuals, or fashion branding.

Create an anaglyph 3D effect — offset red and cyan copies of an image or text to create a stereoscopic illusion (viewable with red-cyan 3D glasses). Use two duplicate layers: one tinted red (left-shifted), one tinted cyan (right-shifted). Use for retro-futuristic themes, movie promotions, or nostalgic design.

patterns.name: Noise Mesh / Grain Gradient

prompts.textual: Create a noise mesh gradient — a colorful gradient with heavy grain texture overlay using mix-blend-mode: overlay. The grain adds an analog, risograph-printed quality. Use higher grain opacity than subtle noise (30-50%). Popular in editorial design, album art, and creative branding. The grain breaks up the digital smoothness and adds warmth.

Create a wireframe/blueprint aesthetic — all elements rendered as thin outlines on a dark background with no fills. Use cyan, teal, or green line colors reminiscent of technical drawings or architectural blueprints. Include grid lines in the background. Use for tech/engineering brands, loading states that reveal wireframe-to-final, or developer-focused design.

Create a data moshing/glitch art effect — simulate video compression artifacts with horizontal color banding, skewed rectangular blocks, color channel bleeding, and blocky macro-artifacts. More aggressive than simple glitch text. Use for experimental art, music visuals, fashion/streetwear brands, or creating a sense of digital decay and disruption.

Create ASCII art — images and designs composed entirely of text characters (letters, numbers, symbols). Use monospace fonts. Can range from simple box drawings (╔═╗║╚═╝) to complex character-based portraits. Use for terminal/CLI aesthetics, hacker themes, retro interfaces, or creative text-based branding.

prompts.textual: Create a frosted acrylic material (Windows Fluent Design style) — similar to glassmorphism but with a visible noise/luminosity texture layer (acrylic noise), very high blur (30-60px), and a more opaque tinted base. Less transparent than liquid glass, more like looking through frosted plastic. Include the subtle luminosity noise overlay that distinguishes acrylic from plain blur. Use for sidebars, navigation panels, or app chrome.

Create an animated gradient border glow — a rotating conic gradient used as a border that cycles through colors continuously. The card's background sits inside, creating a glowing animated edge. Use filter: hue-rotate() animation for continuous color cycling. Use for featured cards, premium subscriptions, AI-generated content indicators, or attention-grabbing CTAs.

Create a Dynamic Island-style UI element (Apple iPhone style) — a pill-shaped floating container that morphs size and shape to display contextual information. Starts as a small pill, expands smoothly to show media controls, timers, directions, etc. Use CSS transitions with cubic-bezier easing for the fluid morphing. The island shape-shifts between compact and expanded states. Use for notification systems, status indicators, or contextual mini-UIs.

Use Intersection Observer API to detect when elements enter the viewport, then trigger CSS animations (fade-up, slide-in, scale-in). Stagger child elements for cascade effects. Libraries: GSAP ScrollTrigger, Framer Motion, AOS. The modern standard for landing page polish.

In CSS, simulate with overlapping blurred circles. In Figma, use the mesh gradient plugin. In tools like meshgradient.com, generate SVG mesh gradients. Each color point influences its surrounding area. The dominant background trend for modern SaaS, AI products, and creative sites.

prompts.textual: Create scroll-linked animations (Apple product page style) — video or animation playback controlled by scroll position. As user scrolls, a product rotates, explodes into parts, or transitions between features. Use CSS scroll-timeline, GSAP ScrollTrigger with video scrubbing, or frame-sequence images. The pinnacle of landing page storytelling — scroll becomes the playhead.

Create glowing orbs/auras — large, heavily blurred radial gradients placed in the background of dark mode interfaces. They create a sense of deep, ambient lighting and high-tech energy. Essential for modern AI and Web3 landing pages.

Create a chromatic aberration effect — simulate a flawed camera lens by splitting an element into its red, green, and blue channels and slightly offsetting them. Creates a subtle glitchy, high-tech, or cinematic feel. Use sparingly on typography or image edges.

prompts.textual: Design with Bubbly/Blobby UI — use extreme border-radii, organic blob shapes instead of strict rectangles, and soft, friendly colors. Everything feels inflated, soft, and approachable. A reaction against strict, sharp-edged corporate design.

categories.name: Texture & Material Simulation
categories.description: Advanced material effects that simulate physical surfaces and substances.

prompts.textual: Create a liquid/fluid effect — organic blobs that morph, merge, and split like a lava lamp. Use animated border-radius values on colored shapes, or SVG with feMorphology filters. CSS approach: animate border-radius between different asymmetric values. Use for creative backgrounds, loading states, or avant-garde hero sections.

Create layered frosted glass — multiple glass panels at different depths with varying blur amounts. Panels closer to the viewer have higher blur and more opacity. Creates a rich, multi-dimensional composition. Use for complex dashboard overlays, creative hero sections, or multi-panel UI designs.

Create a rubber/silicone material effect — soft, matte surface with diffused highlights and shadows. Use inner shadows for the concave/convex surface, and a colored external shadow. Higher border-radius for a puffy feel. The surface should feel squeezable and tactile. Use for playful brands, toy aesthetics, or soft-touch UI elements.

Create an ice/crystal effect — translucent, cool-blue tinted surface with sharp specular highlights and refraction hints. Use light blue gradients with white highlight spots, thin bright borders, and subtle inner glow. Can add fracture lines for cracked ice. Use for winter themes, cold beverage brands, or fantasy/gaming frost elements.

patterns.name: Velvet / Suede

prompts.textual: Create a velvet/suede texture — deep, rich color with very fine grain and a subtle directional sheen. The surface should feel soft and plush. Use high-frequency noise at very low opacity over a deep saturated color. Add a slight gradient suggesting the nap direction. Use for luxury brands, fashion, or premium product backgrounds.

Create a neon sign effect — glowing text or shapes that look like bent neon tube lighting. Use multiple layered text-shadows or box-shadows in the element's color at increasing blur radii. The innermost glow is brightest, outer glows are softer. Use a thin or script font for authenticity. Add a subtle flicker animation. Use for nightlife, restaurant, or retro-themed designs.
prompts.html: Open

prompts.textual: Create a glassmorphic button — a CTA with frosted glass material: semi-transparent background, backdrop blur, thin luminous border, and a highlight line at the top edge. Should feel elevated and premium. Hover state: increase background opacity slightly and enhance the glow. Use for hero CTAs over gradient/image backgrounds.
prompts.html: Glass CTA

prompts.textual: Create an embossed/blind-stamped effect — text or shapes that appear pressed into or raised from a paper/leather surface with no ink. Use opposing light/dark shadows on a background-matching color. The element is defined only by its shadows, not its color. Use for luxury stationery, premium packaging mockups, or elegant watermarks.
prompts.html: PREMIUM

patterns.name: Leather

prompts.textual: Create a leather texture — rich brown/black base with a subtle, bumpy noise filter and an inner shadow for depth. Add a dashed border just inside the edge to simulate stitching. Classic skeuomorphic material for premium, classic, or automotive themes.

Create a holographic foil material — bright, pastel rainbow gradients with a sharp, diagonal white highlight sweeping across it. Simulates shiny, iridescent trading cards or premium packaging. Very popular in modern streetwear and beauty branding.

patterns.name: Knitted Sweater

prompts.textual: Create a knitted sweater texture — a repeating pattern of small V-shapes or interlocking loops, usually in warm, cozy colors. Use for winter promotions, holiday themes, or brands emphasizing comfort and warmth.

categories.name: Social & Marketing Design
categories.description: Design patterns specific to social media, advertising, and marketing materials.

prompts.textual: Design for story/reel format (9:16 vertical, 1080×1920). Full-screen mobile canvas. Key elements: user avatar top-left, brand text safe within center 80%, swipe-up CTA at bottom, progress bar at top. Design for 3-5 second attention spans. Use bold text, high contrast, and motion. The dominant social media format.
prompts.html: Swipe up

prompts.textual: Design a social card / OG image (1200×630px) — the preview that appears when sharing a link on social media. Include: bold title text, brand logo, vibrant background, optional subtitle. Keep text within the center safe zone (text may be cropped on different platforms). High contrast, readable at small sizes. This image represents your content across Twitter, Facebook, LinkedIn, Slack.

Design for standard banner ad sizes: Leaderboard (728×90), Medium Rectangle (300×250), Skyscraper (160×600), Large Rectangle (336×280), Mobile Banner (320×50). Each size needs: headline, visual, CTA button, logo. Design with clear hierarchy — the CTA must be instantly visible. Keep copy under 5 words per line. Follow IAB guidelines.
prompts.html:
728×90
160×600
300×250

prompts.textual: Design an email header/hero — the top section of a marketing email (600px max width standard). Must work without images (many clients block them). Include brand logo, headline, and CTA. Use inline CSS (no external stylesheets). Test across Gmail, Outlook, Apple Mail. Keep important content within 600px width and above the 300px fold line.
prompts.html: Newsletter

prompts.textual: Design a testimonial/review card — star rating (filled stars), quoted review text in italics, reviewer name and photo/avatar, optionally their role/company. Use large quotation marks or a left border accent. Social proof that builds trust. Place near CTAs and pricing for maximum conversion impact.

prompts.textual: Design a pricing table — 2-4 plan cards side by side. Highlighted/recommended plan is taller, has a colored border, and a "Most Popular" badge. Each card: plan name, price, billing period, feature list with checkmarks, and CTA button. Use the Von Restorff effect to make the preferred plan stand out. Anchor with the highest price visible.

prompts.textual: Design a CTA section — a dedicated banner/block designed to convert visitors. Include: compelling headline, brief supporting text, prominent button, and optional trust signals (guarantee badge, "No credit card required"). Use contrasting background from surrounding sections. Place at strategic scroll positions (after features, after testimonials, at page bottom).

prompts.textual: Design a feature showcase block — icon/illustration on one side, headline + description on the other. Alternate layout direction (image-left then image-right) for visual rhythm. Each feature gets: icon in a colored container, bold title, 1-2 line description. The icon container uses a tinted version of the brand color. Standard SaaS landing page pattern.
